264 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 28, 29.

SECTION 28.
JURISDICTION OF COUNTY COURTS.

a. *Bastardy proceedings are civil proceedings.* It would seem that proceedings in cases of bastardy are regarded as civil proceedings, by being placed among that class in the civil code, sec. 28, subdivision 4. (Crenshaw, J.: Conden v. Commonwealth, Ms. Opin., December, 1855.) Chandler v. Commonwealth, 4 Met. 66.

b. *When administration granted on estate of non-resident.* To give jurisdiction to a county court to grant administration of the estate of a person dying in another state, it is necessary to show that, at the time of the death of such person, he had some property, or, at least, a debt owing to him, in the county in which the application is made. The simple assertion of a claim will not give such jurisdiction. Simpson, J.: Wickliffe v. Mason, Ms. Opin., October 1857.

c. *Trial in bastardy case—appearance of defendant not necessary.* Under the Revised Statutes, chap. 6, an appearance by a defendant is not necessary to authorize a trial of a bastardy case. It is not a criminal, but a civil, proceeding. Chandler v. Commonwealth, 4 Met. 66.

d. *Same—how judgment enforced.* Where a defendant in a bastardy case fails to appear, in compliance with his recognizance, the court is not thereby deprived of its jurisdiction over the case, but may give relief, by trial and judgment, against the defendant upon his failure to appear, and, by an attachment, to compel performance of the judgment. *Ib.* 66.

e. *Bastard father liable, though infant.* That the father of a bastard child is an infant, does not relieve him from prosecution, under the statute, for its support. Infants are liable for their tortious acts. But a guardian *ad litem* should be appointed to defend him. *Ib.* 66.

f. *Bastardy cases are special proceedings—not actions.* Under the provisions of the civil code, a bastardy case is a special proceeding, and not an action. It is not for a penalty or forfeiture, nor is it for the enforcement of a private right, within the meaning of the code. *Ib.* 66.

g. *Evidence of a promise of marriage is not admissible on the trial of a bastardy case.* *Ib.* 66.

h. *Appeals do not lie to circuit court, from judgment of county court—when.* The circuit court has no jurisdiction of an appeal from a judgment of the county court, upon a forfeited recognizance for the appearance of a defendant in a bastardy case. An appeal from such judgment is exclusively cognizable by the court of appeals. Commonwealth v. Taphorn, 3 Met. 71.

SECTION 29.
JURISDICTION OF JUSTICES OF THE PEACE.

i. *Plaintiff may credit claim, so as to give justice jurisdiction.* The holder of a note has a right to give a credit thereon, for the purpose of reducing the amount thereof within the jurisdiction of a justice. Stites, J.: Clark v. Ensor, Ms. Opin., January, 1865.

j. *Provisional remedies embraced by sec. 29.* Sec. 29, civil code, conferring exclusive jurisdiction "in all actions and proceedings where the matter in con-

It also states that an infant father is still liable for child support, with a guardian ad litem to be appointed.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Civil Code of Practice discusses rules related to the assignment of choses in action, specifically addressing bills of exchange, equitable ownership, and the assignability of various types of claims. It clarifies that bills of exchange are not subject to set-off in the hands of an assignee and that the legal owner must be a party to a suit if the plaintiff has only an equitable right. It also outlines what types of choses in action can be assigned, including claims by assignees for creditors and debts due to partnerships, with exceptions and conditions.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases lists various types of rights, interests, and causes of action that are not assignable. It details what cannot be legally transferred from one party to another, such as the beneficial interest of a cestui que trust in certain cases, pensions for revolutionary services, mere expectancies, causes of action for torts and personal injuries, rights related to wagers, deceit, malicious abuse of process, excessive distress, fraudulent representations, usury, seduction of a servant, interests not passing to personal representatives, claims for trespass, rights to set aside deeds obtained by fraud, claims for partnership account balances, rights of re-entry, and money lost in gaming. It also mentions Indian annuities, contracts with the US government, and contracts for convict labor. Finally, it states that a person cannot assign a debt against themselves and that an assignment of a demand by someone under sentence of imprisonment is void.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases and discusses the legal concept of assignability of rights and causes of action. It lists various situations where a right or claim cannot be transferred from one party to another. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Civil Code of Practice discusses the transfer of rights and negotiable instruments, specifically focusing on how written instruments for the payment of money, even if payable 'to order,' can be transferred by delivery. It clarifies that such instruments are not negotiable unless indorsed, but delivery is sufficient for the holder to sue in their own name. It also addresses the consideration for assignment, stating that an assignee is not bound to show valuable consideration, and that assignments are subject to set-offs or defenses existing at the time of assignment.

classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses who can bring or be sued in an action, focusing on concepts like assignees, executors, administrators, deputies, partners, and public officers. It details rules for joinder of parties, including situations where some can sue for all, and when creditors with distinct demands can join. Notably, it states that all persons interested in slaves are necessary parties to a suit for their distribution.

classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses procedural rules related to parties in civil suits, specifically concerning executors, administrators, assignors, and assignees. It clarifies who needs to be a party to an action, particularly in cases involving accounts and the assignment of claims. It also touches upon the definition of 'defendant' for jurisdictional purposes and the ability to join multiple holders of fraudulent certificates in a single action.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases. It details rules of civil procedure regarding parties to lawsuits, such as executors, administrators, and assignees. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code.

It details who must be included as a defendant in different types of lawsuits.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses procedural rules related to civil lawsuits, specifically concerning parties to a mortgage foreclosure action, adverse claims, setting aside mortgages, negligence in railroad cases, torts (slander), staying judgments, and principal-surety liability. It does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses rules of joinder in civil actions, specifically concerning partners and their liabilities, as well as receivers. It details when multiple parties can be sued jointly in one action based on the nature of their liability and the instruments involved. It also addresses the joinder of personal representatives of deceased partners and receivers of banks.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867, specifically Section 36, deals with parties united in interest in civil lawsuits. It outlines rules for when joint owners of land can bring a joint action, when parties united in interest must join in a suit, and the right of a sued party to require jointly liable individuals to be made co-defendants. It also addresses situations where joint debtors reside in different states and exceptions to the rule that only jointly liable persons can be joined as defendants. The text includes case law citations to illustrate these points.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil Code of Practice in 1867. It discusses rules of civil procedure regarding parties to lawsuits, specifically focusing on individuals who are 'united in interest.' The content is purely procedural and does not contain any language, provisions, or implications related to racial segregation, discrimination, or the enforcement of Jim Crow policies. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: SECTION 37. PARTIES NUMEROUS.
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code outlines rules for when a lawsuit can be brought or defended by one or more parties on behalf of a larger group. This applies when the parties are too numerous to be brought before the court individually, or when they share a common or general interest. It also addresses situations involving trustees representing numerous beneficiaries.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases from 1867. Section 37 specifically addresses procedural rules regarding parties in lawsuits when there are numerous individuals involved. It details how one person can sue or defend for the benefit of all if they have a common interest or if the group is too large to practically include everyone. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify this as a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with full confidence, and the category is null. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of practice.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 discusses rules regarding parties in civil actions, specifically concerning unincorporated associations, creditors' suits, and joint obligations. It details when a single party can sue on behalf of a group, the requirements for suing on behalf of an association, and how creditors can pursue actions related to assignments for the benefit of creditors. It also touches upon the common law and code provisions for handling joint obligations after the death of one obligor.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Kentucky Code of Practice in Civil Cases discusses rules for joining parties in legal actions, particularly concerning bills of exchange, promissory notes, and surety bonds. It outlines when courts may determine controversies without all potentially interested parties present and when new parties must be brought in. It also addresses situations involving joint ownership and garnishees.

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 40. 289

plaintiff, and the court should require such person to be brought before the court. Forepaugh, etc. v. Appold, 17 B. Mon. 632.

a. Non-resident parties. Notwithstanding the 36th section, Revised Statutes, page 699, provides, where an appeal is taken from the decision of the county court, in the matter of the probate of a will, that "all necessary parties shall be brought before the court," yet, when the record shows that the devisees who are not before the court are non-residents, and may have (by the 38th section, *supra*) a re-trial of the question of probate within three years, and that the controversy may, therefore, be determined, without prejudice to their interest, the failure to make them parties is not ground of reversal. Horton v. Horton, Ms. Opin., December, 1857.

[Sec. 40, Kentucky code, and the first part of sec. 122, New York code, are substantially the same.]

b. By the phrase, "when a complete determination of the controversy can not be had without the presence of other parties," is meant that there are persons not parties, whose rights must be ascertained and settled before the rights of the parties to the suit can be determined; and there are other cases in which a defendant may require other parties to be brought in for the protection of his rights; but this is his privilege. He may waive it. McMahon v. Allen, 12 How. 39.

c. In an action against several, for a specific performance of their joint contract, to purchase real estate of the plaintiff, and secure a part of the price by their bond and mortgage, all of them must be served with the summons, or appear in the action, to enable the court to render a judgment which will be a complete determination of the controversy; and if the action is brought to trial upon the answers of some of the defendants only, when the others have not been served with process, or appeared in the action, the court will not permit the trial to proceed. Powell v. Finch, 5 Duer, 666.

d. When it appears that the presence of other parties than those before the court, is necessary to the complete determination of the controversy, it is the imperative duty of the court to order such persons to be made parties to the action (Davis v. Mayor, etc., of New York, 2 Duer, 663; Shaver v. Brainard, 29 Barb. 25). Although such parties may be non-residents (Sturtevant v. Brewer, 17 How. 571; 9 Abb. 414). *Semble*, where parties are ordered to be brought in, and the cause stands over in consequence, the defendant is not entitled to any costs—he should have demurred (Mitchell v. Bailey, 3 Madd. 61); and the order may be made at any stage of the action (State of N. Y. v. Mayor of N. Y., 3 Duer, 121); before judgment (Carswell v. Neville, 12 How. 445). But the court will not order new parties defendant to be brought in against the will of the plaintiff, unless the presence of such new parties is necessary to the determination of the action. Sawyer v. Chambers, 11 Abb. 110.

e. This section must be confined to actions for the recovery of real or specific personal property (Judd v. Young, 7 How. 79); and it does not extend to an action on contract to recover money (*id.*); nor to an action in the nature of a creditor's bill (Tallman v. Hollister, 9 How. 508); nor to an action to wind up a partnership (Dayton v. Wilkes, 5 Bosw. 655). A partition suit is within this provision; and in such a suit the court may order a person not a party, but hav-

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Kentucky Civil Code, specifically notes to Section 42, discusses the legal concept of interpleader. It outlines the conditions under which a party (often a stakeholder or a defendant facing multiple claims) can bring an action to have adverse claimants brought into court to settle their competing claims to a fund or property. The text cites numerous case precedents to illustrate these conditions, such as the stakeholder not being a wrongdoer, being ignorant of the rights of claimants, and the nature of the dispute (e.g., a disputed wager or a disputed assignment). It also provides examples of court cases where interpleader was granted or denied.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the "Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867" and discusses the legal procedure of interpleader. This procedure allows a neutral party who is holding money or property claimed by multiple other parties to bring those parties into court to resolve the dispute. The text focuses entirely on the legal mechanics and case law surrounding interpleader and does not contain any mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 49. 293

the husband has received property by the wife. But if a recovery is had against them, the judgment as to the husband must be, to be levied only upon property that came to him by his wife, or that might thereafter come. Fultz v. Fox, 9 B. Mon. 502.

a. *Suit on note assigned by married women.* A widow, holding a note by gift, *causa mortis*, married, and subsequently assigned the note to another, who brought an action upon it, making the wife a co-plaintiff with him in the action. It was not alleged that the assignment by her was made with her husband's assent or authority. *Held*, that the petition should be dismissed without prejudice. Turpin v. Thompson, 2 Met. 420.

b. *Wife can not sue husband for possession of her separate estate.* The wife can not sue the husband to recover possession of slaves devised to her as her separate property, free from the control of her husband, which he refuses to deliver to her; no other ground of relief, legal or equitable, being alleged. Matson v. Matson, 4 Met. 262.

c. *Wife suing without husband, no next friend necessary.* The only effect of sec. 49, is to dispense with the necessity for the intervention of a next friend, where the action concerns the separate property of the wife, or where she sues in equity to enforce some equitable right against the husband. *Ib.*

d. *Husband trustee of wife's separate estate.* Where property is given to a married woman for her separate use, without the intervention of a trustee, the husband is regarded as the trustee, holding the legal title for her sole use, and a court of equity will hold him accountable for any violation of the trust; but his mere possession, nothing else appearing, is not sufficient to show such abuse. *Ib.*

e. *Sec. 49 confers no new right on married women.* The provision of sec. 49 of the civil code, that, where the action concerns the separate property of the wife, or where the action is between herself and her husband, *she may sue alone*, relates merely to the form of procedure, and confers no new right of action. *Ib.*

f. *Litigation between husband and wife to be discouraged.* Although there are cases in which a court of equity will lend its aid in the adjustment of conflicting claims arising between husband and wife, with respect to their property, yet it is the policy of the law rather to restrict than to enlarge this class of cases. The necessity must be made clearly apparent. *Ib.*

g. *Suit against wife—amending pleadings.* Husband brought action against wife for sale of her real estate, to which she answered consenting, but petition described the property incorrectly. He amended, making proper description, but wife made no answer to the amendment—*Held*, that a judgment and sale of the lot described in the amendment were void. Bacon v. McDowell, 1 Duv. 74.

[Sec. 28, Ohio code, is similar to sec. 49, Kentucky code.]

h. A *feme covert* is not competent to enter into contracts so as to give a personal remedy against her. She may become entitled to property for her separate use, and pledge the same, and make it answerable for her engagements; but no personal decree can be made against her. The court must proceed *in rem* against the property. Grone v. Frondhof, 3 Law Gaz. 151.

i. Where lands of a married woman have been sold for delinquent taxes, she

entries.ocr_text:
294 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 50.

may, in conjunction with her husband, institute proceedings to redeem, at any time during the coverture. Plumb v. Robinson and wife, 13 O. St. 298.

a. The fact that she is still under the disability of coverture, presents no obstacle to her right to institute this proceeding. "The statute has never been so construed as to prevent a person laboring under disability from suing at any time during disability." (Angel on Limitations, 239, sec. 195.) Per Scott, J.: *Ib.* 305.

b. Upon the hearing of such application, parol evidence is admissible to show that the legal title to the premises, though held by another at the time of tax sale, was so held in trust merely, for the benefit of the applicant. *Ib.* 298.

c. Where a promissory note is given to a woman, either before or during coverture, she retains her interest in it, as payee, until the assertion of her husband's marital rights. Hoop and Hoffman v. Plummer, 14 O. St. 448.

d. And where she sues upon such note by her next friend, during coverture, if no objection be raised by demurrer or answer to her capacity to sue alone, or to the non-joinder of her husband in the action, she may properly recover judgment. *Ib.* 448.

## SECTION 50.

### WHEN WIFE MAY DEFEND.

e. *Wife having an interest, may defend separately.* Where husband and wife are sued jointly, the wife will be allowed to defend separately where she alleges an equitable right to an allowance in the property sought to be subjected. Stites, J.: Ferris v. Parsons, Ms. Opin., January, 1856.

[Sec. 29, Ohio code, is the same as sec. 50, Kentucky code.]

f. In an action against husband and wife for an assault and battery, committed by her, she can not control the management of the defense, or a compromise of the action, against the wishes of her husband; and if he confess judgment for himself and wife, the court will not interfere, at her instance, on the ground that she did not consent, or that she objects to the compromise. Coolidge and wife v. Paris, 8 O. St. 594.

g. The wife may defend in her own right, in cases where her separate rights, as distinct from those of her husband, are recognized and passed upon by courts. Her inchoate right of dower in premises mortgaged by her and her husband—her interests and rights in her separate property, and in the property which came to the husband by her, are all the subject of frequent adjudication, and in such cases she may defend in her own right. *Ib.*

h. But when the action involves no separate right of the wife, and no order or judgment can be rendered except a joint one, affecting both jointly, sec. 29 does not apply. *Ib.*

i. A married woman who is made a defendant in an action with her husband, may defend separately by attorney. Graf v. Wirthwein, 1 Handy, 19.

j. A mere contingent interest of the wife to dower, is no ground for joining her with her husband, as a defendant, in an action for a specific performance of a contract for the sale of real estate. *Ib.*

classification.title: SECTION 50. WHEN WIFE MAY DEFEND.
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice outlines the circumstances under which a wife can defend a legal action separately from her husband. It covers situations where she has an equitable right to an allowance in the property being subjected, where she has separate rights distinct from her husband's, and when she can defend by attorney. It also clarifies when the section does not apply, such as when the action involves no separate right of the wife and only a joint judgment can be rendered.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil Code of Practice in 1867, specifically Section 50, which deals with when a wife may defend a legal action separately. The content focuses entirely on the legal rights and procedural aspects of married women in civil litigation, concerning their property rights and ability to act independently in court. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of practice. The confidence is high because the text clearly defines legal procedures related to marital status and property, with no racial implications.

classification.title: Civil Code - Notes to Sec. 53, 55
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code provides notes and case law interpretations regarding actions by infants and judgments against infants. It details how suits involving infants must be brought (usually in the infant's name by a guardian or next friend), when a guardian can sue in their own name, and the procedural requirements for rendering judgments against infant defendants, emphasizing the necessity of defense by a guardian ad litem after proper service of process.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedures related to minors (infants) in civil cases. It outlines rules for how lawsuits involving minors are initiated and how judgments are made against them, focusing on the role of guardians and the necessity of proper legal process. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: NO PARTY OR ATTORNEY TO BE GUARDIAN AD LITEM
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 outlines rules regarding the appointment and duties of a guardian ad litem for infants in legal proceedings. It specifies that a party or attorney involved in the suit cannot serve as the guardian ad litem. It also details that process must be served before a guardian can be appointed and that a judgment cannot be rendered against an infant without defense by a guardian.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of 1867, specifically Section 66, outlines the rules for who can serve legal process (summons, etc.) in civil actions. It details the plaintiff's right to choose the officer (sheriff, jailor, coroner, constable), conditions under which an officer with an interest cannot serve, requirements for revenue stamps on summons, and rules for amending summons. It also includes notes on defending infants in legal actions.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses rules for serving legal process, including when amendments to proceedings are allowed to prevent a statute of limitations bar, how to handle misnomers in summons, the jurisdiction of the Marshal of the Louisville chancery court, and what constitutes sufficient personal service of a summons. It emphasizes that the object of a summons is to provide fair notice and an opportunity to defend, and any trick to deprive a defendant of this is a fraud. It also touches upon proceedings against absconding debtors via attachment and publication of summons.

classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses the requirements for valid personal service of legal summons and complaints. It details what constitutes proper service, including the delivery and leaving of copies with the defendant, and addresses scenarios where a defendant refuses service or is served through fraud. It also touches upon exemptions from service for witnesses and restrictions on the place and time of service.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses the presumptions and rules surrounding the execution and return of legal process by sheriffs and other officers. It covers scenarios such as the presumption of valid service when a return is marked 'executed,' the implications of incomplete returns, service on infants, amendments to returns, and the invalidity of service by a sheriff who is a plaintiff in the suit. It also details requirements for sheriff's certificates and the effect of out-of-state certificates.

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 76. 301

the name of the sheriff (*Joyce v. Joyce*, 5 Cal. R. 449). A sheriff's certificate of the service of a summons, does not lose its force by lapse of time, or by being previously used for any other purpose; and although it may have been used in reference to a judgment which is afterward vacated, it continues to be proof of the service, and may be used on a subsequent application for judgment. *Brien v. Casey*, 2 Abb. 416.

a. Where a sheriff serves the summons out of his own county, the proof of service must be by affidavit, his certificate of service is of no avail. *Farmer's Loan Co. v. Dickson*, 9 Abb. 61; 17 How. 477.

b. *Disputing the service.* Although it has been said that, the law making it the duty of the sheriff to serve the summons, "whatever he does and certifies in his official duty is evidence, and as between parties to the process, or privies, conclusive evidence, and not liable to collateral impeachment" (*Wheeler v. N. Y. & Harlem R. R. Co.*, 24 Barb. 417); yet it seems to be now well settled that the return of a sheriff or an affidavit of a person acting in his place, or the affidavit of any other person, of the service of a summons, is not conclusive upon the defendant; he may disprove such alleged service, on motion to set the proceedings aside (*Van Rensselaer v. Chadwick*, 7 How. 297; *Litchfield v. Burwell*, 5 *id.* 341; *Wallis v. Lott*, 15 *id.* 567, and see *Bulkley v. Bulkley*, 6 Abb. 307). Where, on a motion to set aside a judgment taken for want of an answer on the ground of a defect in the service of the summons, it appears that the defendant had improperly endeavored to avoid service of the summons, the judgment will not be set aside unless on satisfactory evidence that the summons had not come to the defendant's knowledge (*Southwell v. Marryatt*, 1 Abb. 218). Nor will the court set aside a judgment for non-service of the summons, when it appears that although the defendant knew service had been erroneously made on his foreman for him, and that the plaintiff was proceeding on such service as a regular service, yet did not make his motion to set aside until supplementary proceedings were resorted to (*Hilton v. Thurston*, *ib.* 318). The objection that a summons was not properly served is not available in an answer or demurrer; the remedy is by motion to set aside the service and proceedings thereon as irregular (*Norris v. Hope Mut. Ins. Co.*, 8 Barb. 541). Proof of service on conflicting affidavits. See *Hunter v. Lester*, 10 Abb. 266.

SECTION 76.

[Sec. 65, Ohio code: An acknowledgment on the back of a summons, or a voluntary appearance in court, is equivalent to service.]

c. It is not proper practice for the attorney of the plaintiff to acknowledge service of process for the defendant, and thereby bring him into court. And the court, in such case, will refuse to enter up a judgment. *Sleeper v. Sleeper*, 1 Handy, 530.

d. A party defendant pleading to the action, thereby, by implication of law, enters his appearance in the action, and is estopped from denying the service of process. *Evans v. Iles*, 7 O. St. 233.

e. A defendant, by filing a demurrer to the petition, is estopped from denying the service of process, unless the authority by which this is done can be impeached. *Ib.*

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 88 TO 92. 303

a *Service on infant only, presumed over fourteen, unless contrary appear.* In a proceeding against an infant, where nothing appears in the record to show the age of the infant, or whether he had a guardian or not, the sheriff's return upon the summons, that he executed the same by delivering the infant a copy thereof, will be taken as sufficient, and it will be presumed that the officer performed his duty as required by civil code, sec. 81. The case of Lloyd's adm'r v. McCauley's adm'r, 14 B. Mon. 540, decides nothing inconsistent with this principle. Webber, etc. v. Webber, etc., 1 Met. 18.

b. *No presumption of service on parent or guardian, unless so stated.* A return to process against infants, that it was served by delivering a copy to the persons therein named, negatives the presumption that the service was on anybody, except the infants, and those under fourteen were not before the court. Beverly v. Perkins, 1 Duv. 251.

c. *Return showing service on infant and its mother.* A summons served upon infants and their mother, but which does not state that the infants had no guardian, or that none could be found, it should be presumed, in favor of the sheriff's return, that there was no guardian. Simpson, J.: Simpson's adm'rs v. Dunlap, Ms Opin., December, 1857.

[See. 69, Ohio code, and 81, Kentucky code, are the same.]

d. It is irregular to proceed against an infant until the process has been served strictly in accordance with sec. 69 of the code. Keys v. McDonald, 1 Handy, 287.

e. The summons should show, as a guide to the sheriff, the age of each infant as stated in the petition. *Ib.*

f. Return of the sheriff that he has served the process on the infant alone, is insufficient, unless accompanied with a statement that there could be found no guardian or father, no mother, or person having the care of the infant, or with whom he lived, or that the minor is over fourteen years of age. *Ib.*

g. Where minors are parties defendant, the petition should state their age. The age should also be stated in the summons, so that the officer may be advised in what mode he must serve the process. Morse v. Grames, 2 Law Gaz. 404.

SECTION 88 to 92.

WARNING ORDER—WHEN IT MAY BE MADE.

h. *A return of "no inhabitant,"* does not authorize the clerk to make a warning order under sec. 88 of the code. Thomas v. Webster, Ms. Opin., May, 1853.

i. *Against unknown heirs.* To authorize a warning order under this section, it is necessary to state in the petition that the names of the heirs, or other persons are unknown. It is not sufficient to allege that the heirs are unknown, and the statement must be sworn to by all the plaintiffs. For such an error, this court will reverse. Simpson, J.: Eve v. Riggs, Ms. Opin., December, 1855.

j. *Same—what affidavit must show.* A decree rendered against unknown heirs, upon constructive service, where no affidavit has been made by the plaintiff that their names are unknown to him, upon which to base the warning order, is erroneous. Simpson, J.: Poor, etc. v. Elkins' administrator, Ms. Opin., June, 1857.

806 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 98, 101, 106.

of said estate, where the personal representative was qualified and the settlement made in another county than that in which the action is pending. (Civil Code, sec. 97.) *Ib.* 322.

### SECTION 98.
**ACTION BY WARD AGAINST GUARDIAN.**

*a. Must be brought where guardian qualified.* An action by a ward against his guardian for a settlement of his accounts, for additional security, or for his removal, must be brought in the county in which the guardian was qualified, and the representative of the ward ought to sue in the same court in which the ward is required to sue. *Stone's adm'r v. Powell*, 13 B. Mon. 343.

### SECTION 101.
**ACTIONS AGAINST CORPORATIONS.**

*b. May be brought where there is an agency.* The code of practice, sec. 127 (101), gives a right to sue insurance companies in any county where there is an agency, when the cause of action arises out of a transaction of such agency; and sec. 106 (79) authorizes the service of process upon the chief officer of the agency. *Howard v. Insurance Company*, 13 B. Mon. 285.

### SECTION 106.
**ACTIONS THAT MAY BE BROUGHT WHERE DEFENDANT SUMMONED.**

*c. Some of the defendants must be served in county where action brought.* Where a suit is brought, by ordinary petition, against several defendants, it must appear that some one of them was served with process in the county where the suit is brought, or there can be no judgment against any defendant. *Pottinger v. Mayfield*, 14 B. Mon. 647.

*d. Same.* Such action not defeated by process to another county. The 93d section of the code has no application to a case of an attachment sued out in one county, where defendant is served with process and seeks the sale of land in another. Such proceeding is governed by the 106th section. *Nixon v. Jack, etc.*, 16 B. Mon. 179.

*e. Defendant liable for debt in any county where served.* Wherever the debtor is found, there the debt is owing by him, and he may be sued, and compelled to pay it, without regard to his place of residence. Simpson, J.: *Clemmens v. Vail*, Ms. Opin., December, 1856.

*f. No judgment by default where defendant served out of county.* Sec. 106 of the civil code, which provides that every action, except those enumerated in the previous sections, "may be brought in any county in which the defendant, or one of several defendants, resides or is summoned," does not change materially the former law on the same subject. It does not authorize judgment by default against a defendant served in another county, except where the cause of action is local, or where it affects himself and another served in the county where the action is brought. *Randall v. Shropshire*, 4 Met. 327.

*g. Foreign administrators may be sued in this state.* It is the well-settled doctrine in this state, that an administrator or executor who is appointed or has qualified in another state, and has there received assets into his hands, may be sued in the tribunals of this state, by the person or persons entitled to be same,

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 107, 108. 307

if he shall have removed to, or settled in, this state. (Dorsey's ex'r v. Dorsey's adm'r, 5 J. J. Marshall, 280; Atchison's heirs v. Lindsey, *et al.*, 6 B. Mon. 86.) Manion's adm'r v. Titsworth, 18 B. Mon. 597.

a. *Service procured by fraud not sanctioned.* The courts will not sanction any attempt by fraud or misrepresentation to bring a party within the jurisdiction (Carpenter v. Spooner, 2 Sand. 717; 2 Code R. 140; 3 *id.* 23; Goupil v. Simonson, 3 Abb. 474); thus where by a false statement, made for the purpose, a defendant was induced to come within the city of New York, and was there served with a summons in an action in the superior court, the service was set aside. *Id.*

b. *Service out of the jurisdiction—waiver.* In an action in the common pleas, the defendant (a non-resident) was served with the summons out of the jurisdiction of the court, and he without objection or reservation gave notice of appearance, and then moved to set aside the proceedings for want of jurisdiction of his person; the court held that the defendant, by appearing voluntarily and without objection, had conferred jurisdiction. Smith v. Dipeer, 2 Code R. 70.

SECTION 107.

NO JUDGMENT UPON SERVICE IN ANOTHER COUNTY.

c. A suit was brought in the county of defendant's residence, and process was issued to that and an adjoining county, and served in the latter county, in time for judgment, but not in the former: held, it was error to render judgment upon the service in the foreign county. Raymon v. Reed, 16 B. Mon. 350.

SECTION 108.

WHEN PLAINTIFF ENTITLED TO JUDGMENT ON SERVICE IN ANOTHER COUNTY.

d. *No cause of action against party summoned in the county.* Before a judgment can be rendered against a defendant served with a summons in a different county from that in which the action was brought, some disposition should be made of the action as to the defendants served with the summons in the county in which it was brought; and if it appears from the petition that no cause of action is set forth against such defendants, it will be erroneous to render judgment against the defendant served with a summons in a different county. (Civil Code, sec. 108.) Fernold v. Speer, 3 Met. 459.

e. *Same.* Where, in an action by the assignee of a claim not assignable by statute, the assignor is made a defendant, and served with process in the county in which the action is brought; this does not authorize judgment against the debtor, served in another county, unless the plaintiff show himself entitled to a recovery against such assignor. *Ib.*

[Sec. 58, Ohio code, provides that where an action is rightly brought in any county, process may be issued to any other county against any one or more of the defendants.]

f. Where two are sued as joint contractors, one of whom resides in the county in which the suit is brought, and the other in another county, and service of a summons is made on each in the county in which he resides, and it turns out that the person residing in the county where the action is brought is not liable, as a joint contractor, the plaintiff ought not to recover against the one residing in the foreign county. Dunn v. Hazlett, 4 O. St. 435.

308 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 111.

a. In such case, the person residing out of the county can be required to answer in another county, upon the ground only, that a joint contractor resides, and is sued, in the county where the action is brought. *Ib.*
b. If the person residing in and the person residing out of the county, jointly plead to the merits, and the court find that they were not joint contractors, and nonsuit the plaintiff, it is error in the court to set aside the nonsuit, with leave to the plaintiff to strike out the name of the person residing in the county, and proceed against the one residing out of the county alone. *Ib.*
c. After such leave granted, it is error in the court to refuse to dismiss the proceedings, for the reason that no service of a summons was made within the county where suit was brought. *Ib.*

## SECTION 111.
## JOINDER OF ACTIONS.

d. *For land and damage for detention.* By the code, plaintiff may unite in his petition "claims for the recovery of specific real property, and the rents, profits and damages for withholding the same." This is a privilege which he may or may not exercise. If a claim for rents, etc., is set up in the action for the recovery of the land, it will be a bar to another and separate action for rents. *Warren v. Lindsey, Mss. Opin. January 5, 1857; Walker v. Mitchell, 18 B. M., 546.*
e. *Same—failure to join, does not bar another action.* Anything which the party had a right to recover—as, for instance, extraordinary expenses incurred in the recovery of the land, and not claimed in the petition—may be sued for, and will not be barred by the former judgment. *Ib. 547.*
f. *Actions upon contract and for fraud can not be joined.* It is improper to join an action upon contract, and one upon fraud, in the same petition; but, if not objected to by motion to strike out in the circuit court (secs. 112 and 113), the objection is deemed to be waived (sec. 114). *Simpson, J.: Forkner v. Hart, Ms. Opin., June, 1856.*
g. *Same.* A cause of action growing out of *contract*, and a cause of action *for fraud*, can not be joined in the same petition. If such misjoinder is not excepted to by defendant (sec. 113), and does not prejudice the substantial rights of parties, the court will disregard the error. (Sec. 161). *Stites, J.: Wilson v. Thompson, 1 Met. 123.*
h. *Joint owners of a single tract of land can not be required to bring separate actions for the recovery of possession against each person in possession, where there are several of them, merely because they hold and claim separate parcels thereof, and have no joint possession; but may bring a joint action against them.* *Woolfolk, etc. v. Ashby, etc., 2 Met. 288.*
i. *New cause of action arising after suit brought, can not be joined.* Secs. 111 to 114, inclusive, of the civil code, which relate to the joinder of actions, refer to causes of action existing at the time of the commencement of the suit, and not to such as arise subsequently. *Taylor v. Moran, 4 Met. 127.*
j. *Several joint trespassers—plaintiff may join all or part.* If several persons jointly commit a tort the plaintiff, in general, has his election to sue all or some of the parties jointly, or one of them separately. This rule has not been changed by the civil code.

classification.title: JOINDER OF ACTIONS
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 outlines rules for joining different types of legal claims in a single lawsuit. It covers scenarios such as joining claims for real property recovery with claims for rents and damages, the consequences of failing to join certain claims, and the inability to join actions based on contract with actions based on fraud. It also addresses joinder in cases of joint trespassers and joint owners of land, and clarifies that new causes of action arising after a suit is filed cannot be joined.

It also specifies that causes of action to be joined must be in favor of all plaintiffs and against all defendants, and belong to the same class, though defendants need not be equally affected.

classification.summary: This excerpt from the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases lists various permissible combinations of causes of action that a plaintiff could unite in a single lawsuit. It details scenarios such as claims for possession of land alongside claims for rent, breach of contract with claims for damages, partition of real estate with creditor's bills, and penalties for ordinance violations with injunctions. It also covers actions for trespass, ejectment, equitable relief, legacies, conversion of personal property, breach of contract for real property, and claims against agents and purchasers. Finally, it discusses how multiple penalties can be included in a single cause of action.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases - 1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Kentucky Civil Code of Practice discusses the rules for stating multiple causes of action within a single complaint. It outlines when separate statements are required, particularly when causes of action accrue through assignment from different parties. It also clarifies that stating several grounds of complaint does not necessarily constitute several causes of action and discusses the remedy for improperly stated causes of action. The text further elaborates on the concept of splitting a cause of action, emphasizing that an entire and indivisible demand cannot be split into multiple lawsuits, and a judgment on a part of such a demand bars subsequent suits for the remainder. The distinction between entire and several demands is explained by their origin in a single act or contract versus different acts or contracts.

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 118. 319

not make him a party. His name must be in the style of the action, which is, the names of the parties, plaintiffs and defendants, at the head of the petition. Crenshaw, C. J.: Wilkes, etc., v. Morehead, Ms. Opin., September, 1856.

*a. No judgment against a party, unless cause of action averred against him.* Although a person's name is mentioned in the caption, or style of the petition, and is served with process, and his name, or a similar one, appears as indorser on the note sued on, yet, if no statement or allegation is contained in the petition, charging the indebtedness or liability upon him, it is erroneous to render judgment against him. Duval, J.: Dodd v. Central Bank, Ms. Opin., December. 1857.

[Plaintiff suing as trustee, assignee, guardian, etc., see *post*, pp. 326 to 329.]

**THE FACTS CONSTITUTING THE CAUSE OF ACTION.**

*b. General principles.* The code of practice requires that the plaintiff's petition must contain a statement of the facts constituting his cause of action, with scarcely any other requisition, but that it be made in ordinary and concise language, without repetition. Hill, for use of Wintersmith v. Barrett, etc., 14 B. Mon. 84.

*c. Same. All forms reduced to one.* The code makes no change in the law which determines what facts constitute a cause of action, except that by reducing all forms of action to the single one by petition, it changes the question, whether the plaintiff's statement of his cause shows facts constituting a cause of action in trespass, assumpsit, or other particular form into the more general question, whether the petition shows a right of action in any form. *Ib.* 85.

*d. Code abolishes forms but not rules of pleading.* The code does not authorize a recovery upon any statement of facts, which, before its adoption, did not authorize a recovery in some form of action, and, therefore, former precedents, rules and adjudications may be resorted to as authoritative, except so far as they relate to the distinctions between the different forms of actions, or to formal and merely technical allegations. *Ib.* 86.

*e. Averments in the alternative—construed against pleader.* When the causes of action are set out in the alternative, one of which is good and the other bad, the pleading must be held bad on demurrer. *Ib.*

*f. Matters coming properly from the other side.* It is not necessary, in an action on a contract made by the defendant, to allege that the defendant had authority to make it. This authority is impliedly admitted by the execution of the contract; and if the defendant labors under any disability, as infancy, or marriage, the fact must be relied on by way of defense. So, in an action against the trustees of a town upon a subscription of stock—the presumption being that the act was regular and legal, inasmuch as they were acting under official responsibility (Louisville v. Hyatt, etc., 2 B. Mon. 177). Shelbyville v. Shelbyville T. P. Co., 1 Met. 54.

*g. When judgment will be reversed although no exception.* That the petition does not state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action is a valid ground of reversal, whether the objection was relied upon in the court below or not. Walters v. Chinn, 1 Met. 499.

[See further as to facts constituting cause of action, *post*, pp. 329 to 332.]

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Kentucky Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses rules of pleading, specifically focusing on what constitutes a cause of action and how it must be averred in a petition. It clarifies that a party must be properly charged in the petition to be subject to a judgment and that the petition must state facts constituting the cause of action in ordinary language. It also notes that the code abolishes specific forms of action but not the underlying rules of pleading, and that former precedents can still be used except for distinctions in forms of action. Matters that are presumed to be valid, such as a defendant's authority to enter into a contract or official actions by town trustees, do not need to be explicitly alleged by the plaintiff. Finally, it states that a petition lacking sufficient facts to constitute a cause of action is grounds for reversal, regardless of whether it was objected to in the lower court.

322 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 118.

would do as well or better for the plaintiff than the plaintiff was doing or could do by his said trade; and the breach assigned is, that the testator and his representatives had failed to pay to the plaintiff any part of the sum of $1,200 per year, that being the annual value of the plaintiff's trade and business—*Held*, that the breach as assigned is insufficient. *Moxley v. Moxley*, 2 Met. 309.

*a. No allegation that agreement was written—presumed verbal.* No judgment can be given on a verbal contract embraced by the statute of frauds even if no defense is made. Where the petition merely alleges "an agreement," without saying it was in writing, and without exhibiting any writing, it shows that it was a verbal agreement. *Hocker v. Gentry*, 2 Met. 464.

*b. Action against assignor—what to be averred.* In a petition against the assignor of a note, which the assignee has failed to recover of the obligor, it is necessary to set out the consideration of the assignment, as well as the reason why the amount was not recovered of the obligor. *Elliot v. Threlkeld*, 16 B. Mon. 344; but see Revised Statute, chap. 22, Sec. 7.

*c. Description of instrument—what sufficient.* A note sued on is not fully described in the petition, as its date was not stated; but its amount, and the time it was payable, are stated, and it is alleged that the defendant is indebted to the plaintiff in the amount thereof, the whole of which is unpaid; and the note is filed with and made part of the petition—*Held*, that the petition is sufficient. *Totten v. Cooke*, 2 Met. 227.

*d. What sufficient to make it part of petition.* When the note is filed with the petition, an averment that the defendant is "indebted to the plaintiff in the sum of $779.87 due by note herewith filed"—*Held*, sufficient to make the note a part of the petition. *Ib.*

*e. Action against acceptor.* In a petition against the acceptors of a draft, it is sufficient to aver that the acceptance was due and unpaid. It is not requisite, as to them, to aver presentment at the time and place of payment, as it would be as to drawers and indorsers. Stites J.: *Hill v. Lindsey, etc.*, Ms. Opin., February, 1856.

*f. Demand—when it need not be averred.* In a petition against Adams & Co.'s Express, to recover the amount of a bill of exchange, intrusted to the company for collection, in which a contract to collect and account is alleged, it is not necessary to aver a demand, after a sufficient time has elapsed in which to account. Wheat, C. J.: *Adams Express v. Pindell*, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

*g. Action against agent—what must be averred.* In such case, the petition should, however, aver, or the plaintiff prove, either that the bill was collected, or that it was of value, or that it could have been collected, and was lost by the failure of the defendants to comply with their contract, to authorize judgment by default, without the intervention of a jury. (Sec. 409.) *Ib.*

*h. Exceptions in agreement sued on, must be negatived.* A covenant of seizin, sued upon, stipulated that it was not to be applied to a certain portion of the land therein described. The petition should have alleged that the land adversely held was not the part excepted in the covenant. Simpson, J.: *Yocum v. Landham*, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

*i. Suit to charge separate estate, property must be described.* A petition which seeks to charge the separate estate of a *feme covert*, should state in what that separate estate consists, in order that the judgment might point out what

**324**
**CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 118.**

fendant, A. J. M., in the presence of divers persons, spoke concerning the plaintiff in this action, and his said testimony, these slanderous words, "Old Jim Crawford swore a lie on the trial between Pa and L.; he swore a lie, and I can prove it, in the case of M. and L.; thereby meaning that the plaintiff had committed perjury in his testimony on the trial of said action, wherefore, etc." *Held*, to be sufficient as a statement of a good cause of action, for a charge of false swearing, under sec. 2, art. 8, chap. 28, Rev. Stat., pages 252-3. Marshall, C. J.: *Crawford v. Martin*, Ms. Opin., July, 1856.

a. *Collateral and immaterial facts.* The failure to state that the person before whom the plaintiff was sworn, in an action for damages, on a charge of false swearing, *was authorized by law to administer an oath*, is not material, where the person's capacity and dignity are named, and he has, in fact, such authority by the general laws of the state.

b. *Pleading in the alternative—remote and proximate cause.* The plaintiff alleged that his eye was injured by having got poison in it, which was deposited in a trough, by the defendant, for a malicious purpose, or by the burning of the animal poisoned thereby, etc. *Held*, bad on demurrer, as being in the alternative, and as to the second alternative, as not being a natural or proximate cause of the act of defendant, and that he is not liable, and that the demurrer can not be said to admit the one cause more than the other. Duvall, J.: *Johnson v. Williams*, Ms. Opin., December, 1857.

c. *Facts essential to the recovery must be stated in the pleading.* In a petition to recover the penalty denounced by Rev. Stat., chap. 19, sec. 1, page 184, it is necessary to allege that the orders and records of the court show that the defendant did appear, etc., as the appearance contemplated by the statute is *not the coming bodily in the view of the judge, but the act done of record*, by which the party improperly represented is injured. Stites, J.: *Gossett v. Bruce*, Ms. Opin., December, 1856.

d. *Breach of attachment bond—what a sufficient averment of.* The attachment bond stipulated that the sureties "will pay the defendants the damages, not exceeding seven hundred dollars, which they may sustain by reason of the attachment in the action, if it is finally decided that said attachment ought not to have been granted." The petition, in alleging a breach thereof, is as follows: "The said suit and attachment were dismissed, by order of said court, and on motion of said R. (surety), and thereby said attachment was found wrongful." *Held*, insufficient. There was no liability until the attachment was finally decided, and the dismissal of the suit, on motion of the plaintiff, did not show the attachment was wrongfully obtained. Duvall, J.: *Griver v. Kroner*, Ms. Opin., October, 1857.

e. *Actions for personal injuries—general averments sufficient.* In actions for personal injuries, resulting from negligence, it is sufficient for the plaintiff to allege in general terms that the injury complained of was occasioned by the carelessness and negligence of the defendant. He is not required to state the circumstances with which the infliction of the injury was accompanied in order to show that it had been occasioned by negligence. An allegation of the extent of the injury, and of the manner in which it was inflicted, has been always regarded as sufficient (2 Chitty on Pleading, 650). *Chiles v. Drake*, 2 Met. 146.

f. *Same. A case stated.* A petition by an administratrix alleged that the

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This excerpt from the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses a legal principle related to how parties should be identified in legal complaints. Specifically, it clarifies that when individuals are suing in an official capacity, their official title must be clearly stated and they must complain as such, rather than simply as individuals. The case cited, Gould v. Glass, illustrates that if a complaint identifies plaintiffs by name with their official title as a mere descriptor (descriptio persona), and they do not explicitly state they are suing in their official capacity, a motion for nonsuit may be granted. The text also touches on the general principles of pleading, emphasizing that a complaint must state all facts necessary for the plaintiff to prove their case and that the defendant has a right to controvert.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a legal note discussing civil procedure and pleading rules. It focuses on the technical requirements of how a lawsuit should be framed, specifically regarding whether plaintiffs are suing as individuals or in an official capacity. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of practice.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses rules of pleading in civil actions. It details what needs to be alleged in a complaint, such as excuses for non-performance of contracts, allegations of assets, and fault. It also covers situations where payment is a matter of defense and when a plaintiff must prove specific conditions for a deed or contract to be valid. Finally, it mentions that matters of judicial notice do not need to be alleged, but municipal ordinances do.

It covers whether to state facts according to their legal effect or as they actually occurred, and the requirement for positive allegations. It also touches on making allegations on information and belief, and when positive allegations are strictly required, such as for injunctions.

classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases provides notes and annotations related to Section 118. It discusses legal actions against dissolved corporations, specifically concerning personal estates and the rights of creditors. It also details the necessary averments for actions of false imprisonment, including the requirement to state material facts and the possibility of alleging special damages. Furthermore, it outlines the elements required in a complaint for false representations made to obtain credit, emphasizing the need for a clear statement of the representation, its falsity, and the intent to deceive.

It details the necessary allegations for a complaint regarding a false warranty, including the existence of the warranty, its falsity, and the purchaser's deception. It also outlines the requirements for pleading and proving fraud in legal cases, emphasizing that fraud consists of intention and must be averred. The text also touches upon the rescission of a contract due to fraud and the vendor's obligation to return consideration received.

classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses the legal action for money had and received. It outlines the conditions under which such an action can be brought, including when a defendant has received money belonging to the plaintiff that should be refunded. It also details specific scenarios such as payments made under duress (e.g., to a carrier for illegal demands), money left with a bailee, money paid by mistake, and money paid in partial execution of a rescinded contract. The text focuses on procedural aspects and legal precedents for civil claims.

classification.summary: This excerpt from the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases provides notes and annotations related to civil procedure, specifically concerning the sufficiency of pleadings in various types of civil actions. It details requirements for averring knowledge, consideration, and specificity in complaints for cases involving implied promises, contract disputes, wrongful dismissal, injuries from animals, misrepresentation, private nuisances, employer liability, and sales of goods. It does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses legal principles related to contract performance, excuses for non-performance (such as sickness, death, or acts of law), and the requirements for pleading in actions for non-delivery of goods or for the price of goods. It details mutual and dependent covenants, conditions precedent, and how to plead rescinded contracts. It also addresses the abolition of pleading the same cause of action in different paragraphs.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Civil Code of Practice discusses rules related to demurrers in civil lawsuits. It covers situations where defects in parties are waived by a general demurrer, when a demurrer is sustained due to another action pending between the same parties for the same cause, and when a demurrer is not the appropriate mode to raise certain objections. It also clarifies that the pendency of a suit in another state does not affect the right to prosecute an action in Kentucky.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases provides notes and interpretations for Section 120, focusing on procedural aspects of civil lawsuits. It covers scenarios such as demurrers being overruled if a party is entitled to any relief, variances between pleadings and exhibits, argumentative pleading, insufficient averments in petitions, the effect of demurrers on insufficient defenses, the application of the statute of frauds, and the handling of barred causes of action. It also addresses situations where a demurrer is filed by one of several defendants and the dismissal of cases for lack of jurisdiction.

classification.summary: This note to Section 148 of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases explains that objections related to the non-joinder of parties, defects of parties, improper joinder of causes of action, coverture, premature commencement of action, lack of real party in interest, or lack of legal capacity to sue are waived if not raised by demurrer or answer. These objections cannot be raised for the first time on trial or appeal.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a legal note discussing procedural rules for civil cases, specifically concerning the waiver of objections if not properly raised through demurrer or answer. It details various types of objections that can be waived, such as defects in parties or improper joinder of causes of action. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Kentucky Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases, specifically notes to Sections 124 and 125, discusses rules of pleading in civil actions. It covers aspects such as demurring and answering simultaneously, the sufficiency of allegations, and the construction of answers, particularly regarding legal conclusions and denials of facts. The text references various court cases to illustrate these procedural points.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases provides notes and interpretations related to Section 125, focusing on the sufficiency of denials in legal pleadings. It details what constitutes a valid denial in an answer to a petition, emphasizing the need for specificity regarding disputed facts, items, or amounts. It also addresses insufficient denials, such as general statements of no indebtedness or lack of knowledge without further qualification.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This excerpt from the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses rules of pleading in civil actions, specifically focusing on allegations of fraud, infancy, and coverture. It details what constitutes a sufficient plea, how new matter in avoidance must be pleaded, and how certain allegations are treated if not replied to. It also touches on presumptions related to a feme covert's intent to bind her separate estate.

It covers topics such as the necessity of pleading notice, the allowance of inconsistent defenses, the admission of facts not denied, and the presumption of payment through lapse of time. The notes are based on case law and provide guidance on how these procedural rules are applied in practice.

It also references Ohio code provisions for comparison.

classification.title: SECTION 126. COUNTERCLAIM.
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code defines and outlines the rules for counterclaims in civil lawsuits. It specifies that a counterclaim can be asserted by one of several defendants and must arise out of the contract or transaction set forth in the plaintiff's petition. It also details requirements for a counterclaim to be valid, including sufficient averments to uphold a petition, and provides examples of valid counterclaims in various legal actions such as those involving injunction bonds, dower claims, warranty breaches, and contract violations.

380 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 126.

set out, and the breach thereof alleged, is a good counterclaim against the value of the fifteen acres, being based upon a good consideration, and not within the statute of frauds. *Johnson v. Ballard*, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

a. *Action on railroad subscription*—damage to land from road, good counterclaim. The defendant was sued for his subscription to the Louisville and Nashville Railroad, and alleged, in defense, that the agents of the company who had obtained the subscription, agreed that he should be allowed, in discharge thereof, any damages he might sustain by reason of the road running through his land, and should be paid any surplus of such damage over and above his stock. He sets forth the damages he sustained, and claims, under the alleged agreement, $1000. *Held*, on demurrer, that the answer contained a valid counterclaim. It shows, upon its face, a cause of action in favor of the defendant against the plaintiff, connected by the alleged agreement with the transaction set forth in the petition, and comes within the spirit and letter of the code. *Louisville and Nashville R. R. Co v. Thompson*, 18 B. Mon. 742.

b. *Mandamus to compel levy of tax*—counterclaim for money already paid not good. In a proceeding by mandamus, to compel a county court to levy money and pay for work done in building a bridge, a counterclaim to recover back money already paid on the work, is not proper. But, if the work has been unskillfully done, and not according to the contract, this may be relied upon as a reason why the defendants should not be compelled to pay the balance of the price. *Anderson County Court v. Stone & Son*, 18 B. Mon. 853.

c. *Action for a battery*—that plaintiff committed first assault good counterclaim. In an action for an assault and battery, the defendant may be the party most aggrieved, and the one that is actually entitled to relief. If so, he could maintain an action against the plaintiff, and, having been sued by him, has a right to seek redress against him by his cross action in the form of a counterclaim. *Slone v. Slone*, 2 Met. 339.

d. *Action for purchase money by administrator*—that he had converted property, not good. A purchaser of personal property at a sale by administrators of the estate of their intestate, can not, in an action against him by the administrators upon the note executed to them for the purchase money, rely, by way of counterclaim, upon the fact that one of the administrators had converted a part of the property to his own use after the sale was made. *Phillips v. Keifer*, etc., 2 Met. 478.

e. *Same*: administrator claiming property as his own. In such action, however, where one of the administrators claimed part of the property as his own, and denied that the purchaser had acquired any right to it by his purchase, the purchaser might proceed against him by cross-petition to recover of him in his individual character for the illegal conversion of the property. *Ib.* 478.

f. *Counterclaim signed and verified by stranger good*. An answer set forth a counterclaim and was filed as an answer and cross-petition of defendants. Although signed and verified by a stranger, it is, nevertheless good as a counterclaim. *Lyle v. Poynter*, 1 Duv. 357.

g. *Counterclaim and cross-petition*—distinction. A counterclaim must be against the plaintiffs or some of them; a cross-petition is allowable when the defendant has a cause of action affecting the subject matter of the original suit. *Wells v. Boyd*, 1 Duv. 366.

It details various scenarios and rulings concerning when a counterclaim is valid, how it should be pleaded, and its relationship to defenses. The provided text includes several case examples (a, b, c, d, e, f) that illustrate these principles, drawing from Ohio and New York legal precedents to define and apply the concept of a counterclaim within the context of civil procedure.

classification.title: NOTE TO SEC. 126. Counterclaim
classification.summary: This note discusses the legal concept of a counterclaim in civil actions, detailing its applicability, scope, and limitations. It references various court cases to illustrate points about when a counterclaim can be set up, what it can include (liquidated or unliquidated damages, equitable or legal defenses), and how it interacts with other legal actions like setoffs and arbitration. It also clarifies that a defendant is not obligated to set up a counterclaim and can pursue a separate action.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses the rules and conditions under which a defendant can set up a counterclaim in a civil action. It details when a counterclaim is permissible, the implications of filing a cross-action for the same claim, and the sufficiency of an answer as a counterclaim. It also addresses between which parties a counterclaim can be set up, particularly in cases involving brokers and assignees.

The text cites various New York court cases to illustrate these legal points.

classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses the rules and limitations regarding setoffs and counterclaims in civil actions, particularly when a chose in action has been assigned. It details when a defendant can use a demand against the assignor as a setoff against the assignee, focusing on the timing of notice of the assignment. It also touches upon setoffs in actions by executors and in cases involving rent and leases.

classification.title: SETOFF
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 discusses the legal concept of setoff, which allows parties in a lawsuit to offset mutual debts. It covers when courts of equity can adjudge setoff, enforce agreements to setoff, and details specific scenarios such as a bank's notes being under par, claims assigned for setoff purposes, equitable setoffs involving insolvency or non-residence, and claims not yet due at the time of assignment.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses the rules and limitations regarding setoffs in legal proceedings. It clarifies when unliquidated damages can be used as a setoff, particularly in cases of insolvency or non-residence of the plaintiff or assignor. It also details the conditions under which a setoff is available against an assignee, emphasizing the importance of the setoff existing before notice of the assignment.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Kentucky Civil Code provides notes and interpretations related to Section 128, which deals with the nature, pleading, parties, and treatment of setoffs and counterclaims in civil legal actions. It clarifies that a setoff is essentially a cause of action and subject to the same restrictions. It also discusses how usury should be treated as a defense or failure of consideration rather than a setoff, and that usury paid to a guardian is not pleadable against a ward. The notes also touch upon setoffs involving multiple defendants and non-resident plaintiffs.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code provides notes and case law interpretations regarding the right of setoff in civil actions. It clarifies conditions under which a setoff can be claimed, such as the claim belonging to the party at the commencement of the action, debts existing in the same right, and limitations on setoffs involving administrators or county treasurers. It also addresses situations where a setoff has been reduced to a judgment or involves specific types of exchanges.
