CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 145. 395

section does not apply to title deeds intended to be relied on as evidence in an action for the recovery of lands. They are not a part of the pleadings, but are evidence only. Marshall, J.: Nolan v. Chiles, Ms. Opin., December, 1853.

a. *Upon failure to file after rule, petition should be dismissed.* If the particulars of an account, which are the foundation of an action, are not filed after the service of a rule for their production, the action should be dismissed. Stites, J.: Smith v. Lewis, etc., Ms. Opin., September, 1855.

b. *Same.* The failure of plaintiff to file a sufficient bill of particulars of an account upon which his action is founded, can only avail the defendant for a dismissal upon a rule taken for that purpose. *Ib.*

c. *Exhibit filed is part of record, but substance must be averred.* When a note upon which an action is founded is filed with the petition, it becomes a part of the pleadings and of the record (Civil Code, sec. 145). This, however, does not dispense with any allegations, on the part of the plaintiff, which are necessary to set forth facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action. 14 B. Mon. 84; Dodd v. King, 1 Met. 430.

d. *Contents of note imperfectly stated—what sufficient.* A note sued on is not fully described in the petition, as its date was not stated; but its amount and the time it was payable, are stated, and it is alleged that the defendant is indebted to the plaintiff in the amount thereof, the whole of which is unpaid; and the note is filed with, and made part of, the petition—*Held*, that the petition is sufficient. Totten v. Cooke, 2 Met. 275.

e. *Same.* When the note is filed with the petition, an averment that the defendant is "indebted to the plaintiff in the sum of $779 78, due by note herewith filed"—*Held*, sufficient to make the note a part of the petition. *Ib.*

f. *Exhibit not considered, if not referred to in the pleading.* A paper containing written evidence of the contract declared on, although in the record, can not be considered, if it be not referred to in the petition. Byassa v. Rees, 4 Met. 372.

g. *On trial, party not limited to his exhibits.* A party is not confined, in the introduction of his evidence upon the trial, to such writings as he has relied on in his pleading, but may offer any other instrument of writing which is relevant, and use it as evidence, having first proved its execution. Haney, etc. v. Tempest, etc., 3 Met. 95.

h. *A protest filed as part of a petition in equity, and referred to as such, is a part of the record without a bill of evidence, and will be so treated on appeal.* Harmon v. Wilson, 1 Duv. 322.

[Sec. 117, Ohio code, and sec. 145, Kentucky code, are the same.]

i. When a petition is filed, claiming a recovery for goods and merchandise sold, the account annexed to the petition, as required by sec. 117 of the code, should contain the particulars of the claim, as well as the description and price of the goods; no general statement, by which several distinct claims are grouped together, and the aggregate of the whole value set forth, can be permitted. Goodheart v. Powers, 1 Handy, 559.

j. The same strictness should be required as would be necessary, under the old practice, in furnishing a "bill of particulars," when demanded by the defendant. *Ib.*

k. A transcript of a record, showing the recovery of a judgment, may be "an

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases provides definitions and interpretations of legal terms used in pleadings. It clarifies the meaning of terms such as 'delivery', 'entry on land', 'sold', 'negligence', 'signed', and 'lease in writing'. It also offers guidance on averments in pleadings, including the necessity of stating delivery and acceptance for deeds, the meaning of 'knowledge of the party', the effect of subsequent ratification on an agent's authority, the validity of allegations made on information and belief, and how to infer relationships like master and servant. Additionally, it addresses when allegations in pleadings are presumed to refer to the time of verification or service.

classification.title: SECTION 147. IRRELEVANT AND REDUNDANT MATTER
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 discusses the rules and procedures for striking irrelevant or redundant matter from legal pleadings. It clarifies when a judgment might be reversed due to such errors, the waiver of objections if parties proceed to trial, and the proper remedies (motion to strike or make definite) versus demurrer for various pleading defects. It also touches on the practice of filing both a demurrer and an answer simultaneously.

entries.ocr_text:
CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 151. 409

## SECTION 151.
### MITIGATION.

**a. What admissible evidence.** It is well settled that matter which would sustain a plea of justification is inadmissible under a plea of not guilty (Litt. Sel. Cases, 150; 1 B. Mon. 170). But matter which falls short of a justification may be admitted in mitigation of damages. *Hart v. Reed*, 1 B. Mon. 170; *Thurman v. Virgin and wife*, 18 B. Mon. 791.

**b. Words charged must be substantially proved.** The rule of evidence in actions of slander formerly was that the plaintiff must prove the precise words. That rule has been no further relaxed than to admit proof of the substance of the words. It is not enough to prove words of the same effect or import, or conveying the same idea. The words must be substantially the same words, and it is not sufficient that they contain substantially the same *charge*, but in different phraseology; equivalent words of slander will not do. *Taylor v. Moran*, 4 Met. 127.

**c. Court to determine whether words admissible.** While the proof of speaking the words is for the jury, the correspondence between the words spoken and laid is for the court. *Ib.*

**d. Words spoken after action brought.** In an action of slander, words spoken pending the action, and set up in an amended petition, were admitted in evidence without objection or any admonition to the jury as to the weight or effect they were to give to them; the court instructed the jury that the plaintiff had a right to recover, as well for slanderous words spoken after as before the action was brought, and that in determining the amount of damages they were to consider *all the facts and circumstances proven in the cause*; the case was submitted to the jury and argued by counsel, after which the court said to the jury, in another instruction, that the plaintiff could not recover in this action for words uttered since the filing of the original petition, but that evidence thereof was admissible on the question of malice, and that they could not regard the words as *substantive slanders*, for which they might give damages in the action. *Held*, that the error was not cured by this caution. It should have been full and explicit as to the purpose for which alone the evidence was admissible, embracing specifically the idea that such evidence could not be considered by them to increase the damages. *Ib.*

[Sec. 125, Ohio code, is substantially the same as 151, Kentucky code.]

**e.** In a suit for defamation, evidence of actionable words, or distinct libels, not declared on, is competent to show the *quo animo*; but they can not be made the foundation for a recovery of damages for any injury done by *such words*, or libels; and so far as they can affect the damages, it is solely by showing the *degree of malice* in the publication of the words or libel declared on. *Van Derveer v. Sutphin*, 5 O. St. 293.

**f.** Matters not capable of supporting an action or constituting a justification, may be given in aggravation or mitigation of damages, as there is no other mode of bringing it before the jury. *Fisher v. Patterson*, 14 O. R. 418.

**g.** The general character of the plaintiff is in issue in an action of libel, and general reputation of particular acts is not admissible. *Ib.*

entries.ocr_text:
410 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 151.

a. Actionable words spoken more than a year before the commencement of suit, and not declared on, are admissible to prove malice. And words not actionable, as well as the acts and declarations of the defendant, are admissible to show malice. *Stearns v. Cox*, 17 O. R. 590.

b. Where a libel is ambiguous as to the person referred to in it, it is competent for witnesses to give their opinion as to the person intended to be referred to in the libel. And the witness may state his general belief and opinion as to the defendant's meaning. The other party may inquire the reasons for such opinion or belief. *McLaughlin v. Russell*, 17 O. R. 475.

c. Under the plea of the general issue in an action for a libel, it is competent for the defendant, with a view to mitigate damages, to give evidence of a general or common report in circulation prior to the publication complained of, that the plaintiff was guilty of the charge imputed to him; but such evidence is only competent to rebut the presumption of malice, and not by way of affecting or attacking the character of the plaintiff. *Van Derveer v. Sutphin*, 5 O. St. 293.

d. Under the plea of the general issue in such action, evidence of particular facts or circumstances calculated to have induced mistake, or to have misled the party in the publication of the slander, is competent in mitigation of damages by way of rebutting the presumption of malice, and that, too, whether the evidence tended to prove the truth of the charge or not. *Ib.*

e. A notice that the defendant will prove the truth of the libelous charge in justification, must contain all the substantial averments of a special plea; and where the libel declared on contains multiplicity of matter, general averments in the plea of the truth of the publication, in order to avoid prolixity of pleading, are not admissible; but the particular acts done, which the defendant relies on as constituting the charge, must be set out, so that the court may determine whether the facts warranted the charge. *Ib.*

f. A plea or notice of justification must aver the truth of the material and substantial charges, or of each substantial and libelous charge, in language as broad as the charge in its full and legal sense; and although where there are separate and distinct charges in the same libel, it is allowable in the same plea or notice, to plead the general issue as to a part, and justify as to the other charges, yet it is essential that a plea or notice should substantially answer the whole count or ground of action declared on. *Ib.*

g. In an action of slander for words spoken against the chastity of the plaintiff's wife, it is competent for the defendant, under the general issue, in mitigation of damages, to prove that the wife and an unmarried man had lived together in one house; where a knowledge of such mode of living had come to the defendant before the speaking of the words. *Reynolds v. Tucker and wife*, 6 O. St. 516.

h. Matters not capable of supporting an action or constituting a justification may be given in aggravation or mitigation of damages, as there is no other mode of bringing it before the jury. *Fisher v. Patterson*, 14 O. R. 418.

i. The general character of the plaintiff is in issue in an action of libel and general reputation of particular acts is not admissible. *Ib.*

j. Circumstances tending to show that the defendant spoke the words under a mistake, or that he had some reason to believe they were true, are admissible to rebut malice. *Wilson v. Apple*, 2 O. R. 270.

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 151. 413

48); the defendant *can not* set up in mitigation former controversies between him and the plaintiff, having nothing to do with the alleged slander (*Lister v. Wright*, 2 Hill, 320), or that the father of the plaintiff shortly before the uttering of the slander, used irritating language toward the defendant (*Underhill v. Taylor*, 2 Barb. 348); or in an action against the editor of a newspaper, that the libel was published on the communication of a correspondent (*Talbut v. Clark*, 2 M. & Rob. 312). Where the libel, as averred in the complaint, charged the plaintiffs, who were contractors with the corporation of New York, with fraudulent attempts to influence the supreme court in determining a suit therein pending against them, and with having bribed the common council—*Held*, that allegations in the answer of a generally wasteful and improper course of conduct on the part of the common council, and that certain contracts obtained therefrom by the plaintiffs, would result in vast profits to them, and in detriment to the city, and that they falsely pretended to possess patent rights for paving, and made other pretenses, and that they poorly performed previous contracts, and had obtained preferences over lower bidders, were properly stricken out with additional similar matter, as too remote in application to the alleged libel, to constitute mitigating circumstances. *Russ v. Brooks*, 4 E. D. Smith, 645.

a. When circumstances which can only be given in evidence in mitigation of damages are set forth in the answer, it must be distinctly stated that it is with that view, and for that purpose only that they are introduced; since otherwise the plaintiff will have a right to infer that they are relied on as a bar to the action, and upon that ground may properly demur to them. *Fry v. Bennett*, 1 Code Rep. N. S. 238; 5 Sand. 54; *Matthews v. Beach*, 5 Sand. 264; *Ayres v. Covill*, 18 Barb. 260.

b. An answer which alleges new matter in justification, may allege the same matter in mitigation (*Howard v. Raymond*, 11 Abb. 155); and the mitigating circumstances may be alleged by a mere statement that the defendant will, on the trial, set up as mitigating circumstances the matter plead in justification. *Id.*

c. Matter pleaded only in mitigation, is not a subject of demurrer (*Newman v. Otto*, 4 Sand. 669; and see *Van Benschoten v. Yaple*, 13 How. 101); and can not, on the trial, be offered in justification. *Baker v. Wilkins*, 3 Barb. 220.

d. Allegations in an answer, in an action for libel, which would not be allowed to be proved, even in mitigation of damages, should be stricken out as irrelevant. *Brown v. Orvis*, 6 How. 376.

e. To a complaint for slander, in charging that plaintiff had stolen defendant's hay, the answer denied each allegation of the complaint, and then, as a separate defense, stated that the words charged as slanderous were spoken in reference to a quantity of hay the defendant had cut and stacked, and to which hay the plaintiff claimed title, and which hay, under such claim of title, and in the daytime, the plaintiff took from the land of the defendant to the adjoining land of him, the plaintiff; which taking, and not the crime of larceny, was what he, the defendant, meant by the words charged, and he was so understood by all who heard him. A motion to strike out this last defense as irrelevant and redundant, and because it was inconsistent with the general denial, was denied. Harris, J., held, that, *perhaps*, the matter set up might have been admissible under the general denial, but that was no reason for striking out matter

classification.title: Allegations of Values
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 discusses how allegations of value and damages are treated in civil lawsuits. It clarifies that such allegations are not automatically taken as true, even if not denied by the defendant, and that proof is often necessary to establish the value of property or services for a judgment. It also notes exceptions where specific sums alleged to be due and owing, or where an article was sold for a stated price, might be taken as true without further proof.

416 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 153.

the counterclaim must be taken as true in this state of the pleadings, unless plaintiff prove the allegation of his reply, it being affirmative matter in avoidance of the answer. Stites, J.: Philips v. Hazlerigg, Ms. Opin., Jan. 1858.

a. *Notice of motion against sheriff—statements to be proved.* Although the defendants may make default, the statements in a notice of a motion against the sheriff and his sureties for failing to pay over money to a county creditor, can not be taken for confessed; but the matters relied upon to sustain the motion must be established by proof. Todd, etc. v. Caines, 18 B. Mon. 622.

b. *Parties constructively served—allegations to be proved against.* The allegations of a petition which seeks to vacate a deed of assignment for the benefit of creditors, upon the ground of fraud, will not be taken for confessed as against creditors therein preferred, where process is not actually served upon the maker of the deed, and there is a failure to prove fraud on his part. Wheat, C. J.: Gordon, etc. v. Jackson, etc., Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

c. *Failure to answer interrogatory.* No judgment can be based upon the failure of the defendant to answer an interrogatory not founded on some material allegation in the petition. The defendant is only bound to answer the allegations of the petition; and when the plaintiff propounds interrogatories to him, the plan prescribed by the code must be pursued. The defendant then becomes a witness, and his answers are made evidence. He is not bound to forego this advantage and answer inquiries, though in the body of the petition, not founded on any allegation. Burnett & Miller v. Garnett & Thompson, 18 B. Mon. 71.

d. *Matters in avoidance not amounting to counterclaim.* Matters in avoidance in an answer and cross-petition, not in the nature of a counterclaim or setoff, can not, for want of a reply, be taken as true. Ashby v. Woodfolk, 3 Met. 540.

ALLEGATIONS TO BE DIRECT AND FACTS FULLY STATED.

e. *Facts to be directly and positively averred.* The rules of pleading require that the existence of the facts necessary to constitute the cause of action should be directly and positively averred, and not set forth merely as admissions which have been made by some other person, or even by the defendant himself. Lee v. Waller, etc., 3 Met. 61.

f. *Same. Notice must be specially averred.* When notice of the acceptance of an offer to guaranty is requisite (in an action on such guaranty), the allegation of notice in the petition should be special, and such as will enable the court to determine from its statements whether or not the notice was given as the law requires (2 Ala. 373; 3 Conn. 438). The general averment, "of all which the defendants had notice," is not sufficient. Steadman v. Guthrie, 4 Met. 149.

g. *Evasive denials—denial of conclusion without controverting facts.* In an action for the hire of a slave, the defendant denies that he had any negro of the plaintiff hired, as charged in the petition, nor did he agree to pay either clothing or hire, and therefore denies that he owes plaintiff $140, or any other sum for the hire or clothing of said negro as charged; but fails to controvert the allegation that he had the negro in his possession for the year stated and had received the product of his services to the amount or worth stated in the petition and due at the time therein alleged. *Held*, that the effect of the answer is, to deny merely the express contract of hiring, as stated in the petition, and to admit the facts charged, upon which the liability of the defendant to pay the value of the

classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses rules of pleading and evidence. It clarifies when allegations in a lawsuit are taken as true (for confessed) and when they must be proven by evidence. Specific points covered include the proof required for motions against sheriffs, allegations against constructively served parties, failure to answer interrogatories, and matters in avoidance not amounting to a counterclaim. It also emphasizes the need for direct and positive averments of facts in pleadings and special averments of notice.

classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Kentucky Civil Code, specifically notes to Section 155, discusses the requirements for pleadings in civil cases. It clarifies that deeds or other writings filed with pleadings are not automatically part of the record. It emphasizes that a petition must clearly state the substance of a contract, its breach, and the plaintiff's entitlement to relief. It also outlines requirements for actions against indorsers of promissory notes and actions based on judgments from other states. The notes reference various case precedents and sections of the Ohio code for comparison.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867, specifically Section 156 and its notes, deals with the legal concept of 'variance' in pleadings. It outlines that the cause of action must be both alleged in the pleadings and established by proof. If there is a discrepancy between what is alleged and what is proven, it can be fatal to a judgment unless the variance is deemed immaterial and the party was not misled to their prejudice. The text discusses how variances are to be handled, including the possibility of amending pleadings.

a. *Same: allowed in actions for slander.* Under this section, a plaintiff will be allowed to amend his petition, in an action of slander so as to allow the use of other slanderous words by the defendant. Simpson, J. : Winn v. Elliott, Ms. Opin., January, 1854.

b. *Immaterial errors: waiver.* That pleadings were not filed by order of court, first objected here, will not reverse. It is no substantive error. Stites, J. : Edwards v. Kendrich, Ms. Opin., December, 1856.

c. *Evasive answer: defendant not allowed to amend after making.* The defendant having answered evasively, ought not to be allowed to amend his answer, after the testimony is closed. Crenshaw, C.J. : Trumbo v. Botts, Ms. Opin., January, 1857.

d. *Nor where he knew the facts at time of making first answer.* Where the defendant must have known the facts contained in an amended answer, which he proposes to file, at the time of the filing of his original answer, it is no abuse of discretion to refuse leave to file it. Stites, J. : Barbour v. Moss' adm'r, Ms. Opin., July, 1857.

e. *Variance—amendment after proof adduced.* In an action against a city for work and labor done on its streets at its instance, the petition alleged the performance of the work; the proof was that the work was done under a special contract that the plaintiff was to look to the lot owners for payment; the court instructed the jury to find for the defendant; after which, but before the verdict was rendered, the plaintiff offered an amended petition, alleging facts sufficient to render the city liable under the special contract. *Held*, that the amended petition should have been permitted to be filed. Kearney v. City of Cov., 1 Met. 339.

f. *Demurrer undisposed of presumed waived.* Where an answer was filed at the same time with the demurrer to the plaintiff's petition the fact that the parties proceeded to trial without objection, and without noticing the demurrer, authorizes the inference that the demurrer was waived or overruled. Besides, the petition presented a substantial cause of action, and the failure to dispose of the demurrer, if it had been excepted to, would not avail for reversal. Danville, etc., T. P. Co. v. Stewart, 2 Met. 119.

g. *Technical objections waived if not made at proper time.* Objection founded on technical rules of practice waived where the conduct of the party entitled to insist on it has been such as to induce his adversary to believe, and to act upon the belief, that the objection had been abandoned. Meader v. Turpin, 4 Met. 93.

h. *Defects in petition cured by answer.* It has long been a well settled rule of practice that defective allegations of the plaintiff's pleadings may be supplied and cured by the answer of the defendant; and there is nothing in the code of practice which can be construed as abrogating or modifying the rule (13 B. Mon. 466, and subsequent cases). The doctrine inculcated by the whole spirit and tenor of the code is, that verdicts and judgments which have been

entries.ocr_text:
428 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 161.

rendered upon substantial issues of fact, fairly presented by the pleadings, should not be disturbed on account of mere technical defects not affecting the merits of the controversy. Civil code, sec. 161; Riggs v. Maltby, 2 Met. 88.

a. *Title of the action may aid defective averments.* Suit by one who is widow and administratrix of an intestate to recover damages against the proprietor of a railroad, for the carelessness, negligence, and unfitness of the employees and agents of the company in running the engine over and killing her husband. The style of the action in the petition is, "E. L., plaintiff, administratrix of M. L., deceased, against R. B. B., defendant." *Held*, that this shows conclusively the character in which she sues, and an amended petition in which it is distinctly averred that the action is brought as administratrix does not therefore change the character of the action. Bowler v. Lane, 3 Met. 311.

b. *Same party both maker and payee in note.* Q. and D. made a note payable to A. and said D. A. sued both makers, abated as to D., and took judgment against Q. *Held*—right. Quesenberry v. Artis, 1 Duv. 30.

c. *Contradictory pleading.* Defendant pleaded that the note sued on contained $160 usury. Plaintiff, in reply, admitted it contained $36 92 interest. Upon the vague statement of a witness tending to show there was no usury, plaintiff was allowed to amend his reply and deny that the note contained usury. *Held*, error. Hubble v. Murphy, 1 Duv. 278.

[Sec. 137, Ohio code, and 161, Kentucky code, are the same.]

d. To prevent the rights of a suitor, or the merits of his cause, from being sacrificed to technical rules, or to the mistakes or omissions of his attorney, are among the prominent objects of the code, hence the section in regard to mistakes, before and after judgment. Irwin v. Bank of Belefontaine, 6 O. St. 81.

e. A mistake in an appeal bond may, by consent of the sureties, be amended, in the appellate court, or a new bond, by way of amendment, may be filed. *Ib.*

f. Where a party has failed in his suit, on account of having mistaken the form of his remedy, the court will not set aside the judgment, and allow him to amend by substituting a form of proceeding under the code, for a form of proceeding not instituted under the authority of the code. United States v. Collier, 6 O. St. 61.

g. Where a defendant answers that he executed the contract, upon which he is sued, as a broker or agent, and after the testimony is before the court, asks to amend his answer so as to show he executed the contract under a mistake of his legal responsibility thereon, the court will not grant leave to amend unless the facts proved show at least a reasonable probability that this can be established. Wilson & Co. v. Bailey & Son, 1 Handy, 177.

h. Striking out the name of the nominal plaintiff, and inserting that of the real party in interest, is not an amendment that changes substantially the original claim made, nor could it, by any means, operate as a surprise to the defendant. Ansonia Rubber Co. v. Wolf, 1 Handy, 236.

i. When leave is asked and granted to file an amended pleading, then is the time to object; if the objection of the party is overruled, he may except to the decision of the court. It is too late after a pleading has been filed on leave without objection. *Ib*

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases - 1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses amendments to legal complaints and answers. It details various scenarios where amendments are permissible, such as striking out a defendant's name, adding a verification, altering the prayer of a complaint, or increasing the amount claimed in an action on contract. It also addresses situations where amendments are not allowed, like when a plaintiff intentionally sues on contract to gain an advantage over suing in tort, or when a plaintiff attempts to incorporate essential facts that occurred after the action commenced. The text also touches upon the defense of usury and the statute of limitations, noting that amendments to introduce these defenses are generally not permitted.

classification.title: SECTION 162. SUPPLEMENTAL PLEADING.
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 outlines the rules for supplemental pleadings. It clarifies that a supplemental pleading can be used to introduce causes of action or circumstances that arose after the initial suit was filed, such as a forfeiture of a mortgage occurring during the pendency of an action to recover possession. It also specifies that slanderous words spoken after the suit commenced cannot be brought in by amendment to establish a new ground of recovery or enhance damages, but may be used to show intent with cautionary directions from the court. The section emphasizes that amendments and supplemental pleadings should relate to the case actually in court and explain or perfect the original cause of action, rather than constituting an entirely new and distinct case.

438 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 168.

the court will not even before trial allow a supplemental answer to set up a technical defense, to defeat a just claim (Hoyt v. Sheldon, 4 Abb. 59; 6 Duer, 661); nor to set up any matter of defense known to the defendant at the time his former answer was put in (Houghton v. Skinner, 5 How. 420). The supplemental answer under the code is a substitute for the old plea of *puis darrien continuance*; but it differs from that plea in this respect, that the supplemental answer may be allowed on motion whenever the facts forming the ground of the answer have occurred since the answer was put in, or where the defendant was ignorant of them at the time of pleading the first answer; whereas the plea of *puis darrien continuance* could strictly be pleaded only before or at the next continuance after the facts transpired. When the facts asked to be incorporated and pleaded in a supplemental answer go to divest the plaintiff of the right to maintain the action, and transfer the cause of action to another, who has received satisfaction for the demand involved in it, it is the duty of the court to grant the motion, whether the motion be made at the earliest day or not (Drought v. Curtiss, 8 How. 56; see however, Morell v. Garelli, 16 Abb. 269, where it is said that the application should be made promptly.

a. On motion for leave to file a supplemental answer the court will inquire into the truth and sufficiency of the proposed answer, and will not grant the leave unless the answer appear to be true in fact, and to state a good defense. Morell v. Garelli, 16 Abb. 269.

b. The putting in a supplemental answer does not necessarily waive the former answer, yet the court may make it a condition of leave to file such supplemental answer, that the defendant waive his original answer (Bate v. Fellowes, 4 Bosw. 630; and see Dann v. Baker, 12 How. 521). A supplemental pleading may be demurred to. Goddard v. Benson, 15 Abb. 191.

SECTION 168.

INTERROGATORIES.

c. *Answers to interrogatories evidence.* A party's answer to interrogatories, submitted by his adversary, is made evidence in the cause, by secs. 167 and 168. Simpson, J.: Bennett v. Bennett, Ms. Opin., December, 1854; Short v. Tinsley, 1 Met. 397.

d. *Party answering may state new matter not sought by the interrogatories.* The civil code not only authorize answers to special interrogatories, to be read by either party as a deposition, but permits the party answering, to go beyond the interrogatories, and state any new matter concerning the same cause of action, which may also be read. Stites, J.: Thompson v. Allen, Ms. Opin., June, 1855.

e. *And opposite party may make counter-statement.* Statement of new matter, in an answer to plaintiff's interrogatories, is allowed, to which the plaintiff may reply by a counter-statement, each statement being admissible. Stites, J.: Brown v. Brown, Ms. Opin., January, 1856.

f. *Statement must be pertinent to the issue.* While a party has the right, in his reply to interrogatories, to state any new matter concerning the same cause of action, any statement which he make concerning any other cause of action should be disregarded. So the party interrogating, has no right, in his rejoinder, to testify except in regard to new matter introduced by his adversary, concerning the same cause of action. Stites, J.: Wynn v. Fisher, Ms. Opin., June, 1857.

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 177, 180, 188, 189, 190. 439

SECTION 177.

a. *Evasive answers.* Where the allegations of the answer are evasive, and fail fairly to respond to the interrogatories, propounded in the petition, in which there were allegations sufficient to sustain the judgment, a failure to prove the allegations of the petition, will not work a reversal. Simpson, J.: Martin and wife v. Fleming, Ms. Opin., June, 1856.

b. *Same.* Where a grantee in a deed of conveyance, alleged to be fraudulent, was called upon by interrogatories, to explain where the consideration expressed in the deed was obtained, and answers in an evasive and unsatisfactory manner, it is strong evidence of fraud and unfairness in the transaction. Simpson, J.: McMicken v. Hammond, Ms. Opin., January, 1857.

SECTION 180.
**GROUNDS OF ARREST.**

c. *Surplusage will not vitiate affidavit.* The fourth clause of sec. 180, does not require the affidavit to state, that the defendant is about carrying out of the state his money, or choses in action, but that he has money, etc., in possession of himself, or of others for his use, and is himself about to depart, etc., without leaving, etc. If the former statement is contained in the affidavit, it does not, however, vitiate it. Marshall, J.: Jones v. Terant, Ms. Opin., January, 1854.

SECTIONS 188, 189.

d. *To loan out money no part of sheriff's official duty.* The sheriff is not the proper officer to loan out money in custody of the court, and it is no part of his official duty to do so, and if he makes such loan, though by order of court, his sureties are not responsible for his failure to repay it. Sanders v. Parrott, 1 Duv. 292.

SECTION 190.
**BAIL—HOW TAKEN.**

e. *Form and sufficiency of bond.* Although this section (190) requires the bond to be executed to the plaintiff, yet the form appended by the codifiers, page 344 (edition of 1854), is deemed sufficient, and must be understood as an undertaking to the plaintiff. Crenshaw, C. J.: Runyan v. Whaley, Ms. Opin., September, 1856.

f. *Bond to perform the judgment of the court, void.* Under the provisions of sec. 14, page 615, of the Revised Statutes, "no sheriff or other officer shall take any obligation of or from any person in his custody, for or concerning any matter relating to his office, otherwise than such as is specially directed by law. Every obligation taken by any such officer, by color of his office, in any other manner or form, shall be null and void." Under an order of arrest, the sheriff, instead of the bond designated by the civil code (sec. 190), took bond, with security, that the defendant would perform the judgment that might be rendered in the action. *Held*, that such bond was null and void. The Lex. & Dan. R. R. Co. v. Barbee, 1 Met. 384.

g. *That plaintiff authorized such bond to be taken, gives it no validity.* That the plaintiff authorized the sheriff, under the process in his hands, to take the

440 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 198.

bond as described, in lieu of the bond required by law, did not render it any the less a bond taken under color of his official authority, or give it any validity. *Ib.* 384.

a. *Not authorized by sec. 186.* The provisions of sec. 186 of the civil code give no authority to the sheriff, under an order of arrest, to take bond, with security, of the defendant, to perform the judgment that may be rendered in the action. *Ib.*

b. *Bond must be executed in presence of officer.* In an action on a bail bond executed by a party in custody under an order of arrest, to enforce the liability of the bail, it is necessary that the petition should allege that the bond was executed in the presence of the sheriff, or other officer authorized to take bail. Civil Code, sec. 190; 4 Bibb, 450; Jones v. Bunn, 2 Met. 490.

c. *Failure to return bond, will not vitiate.* The requirement that the bond, when accepted, shall be returned to the clerk's office, is directory to the sheriff; and his failure to comply with it would not defeat or impair the rights of the obligee. *Ib.* 490.

d. *What necessary to fix liability of bail.* The petition must also allege that the judgment had not been paid or satisfied. An averment that judgment had been obtained against the principal, that he had not surrendered himself or been surrendered by his bail, and that execution had issued and been returned "not found," is insufficient. *Ib.* 490.

e. *Slight informalities do not vitiate.* That the bond of special bail stipulates that the defendant "will render himself amenable to the court thereupon"—the words "process of" being omitted—does not make the bond invalid. In substance and import it is sufficient. *Ib.*; Abbott v. Daniel, 3 Met. 339.

### SECTION 198.

### LIABILITY OF BAIL—HOW FIXED.

f. *Must be valid execution within time fixed by code.* To fix the liability of special bail it is necessary that there should be a valid execution against the body of the defendant, upon the judgment, that it should be placed in the hands of the officer within the time prescribed, and that it shall be returned not found (Civil Code, secs. 195, 198). No other test of legal diligence will suffice. A failure to comply with either of these essential conditions will exonerate the bail. Abbott v. Daniel, 3 Met. 339.

g. *Execution "to pay commonwealth" instead of "plaintiff" insufficient.* Where, in such case, the execution against the body of the defendant was "to satisfy and pay the Commonwealth of Kentucky the sum of," etc., "which was late in our said court adjudged against him for debt in favor of J. B. A.," it is insufficient to fix the liability of the bail. It should be to pay and satisfy the plaintiff in the judgment. *Ib.* 339.

h. *Execution need not be returned within twenty days in order to fix bail.* To fix the liability of the bail in a civil action, it is not necessary that the return of "not found" upon an execution against the body of the principal should be made within twenty days after the judgment against him; the execution must be placed in the sheriff's hands within twenty days from the judgment (Civil Code, sec. 198), but the return day is fixed by the rules of the court whence the execution emanates. Allcorn v. Tuggle, 3 Met. 537.

442 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 207.

a. An action to recover the possession of personal property can be maintained only against him, who has the legal or actual possession of the same, at the time of the service of the order. To enable the plaintiff, where the property has not been taken, to proceed in his action, as one for the recovery of damages only, the action must have been rightly brought in the first instance. It would seem, that the action can be proceeded in to recover damages, only in cases where the defendant, having the legal possession of the property, prevents the taking of it by the officer, whether by force or by concealing it. *Allen v. Leget*, 1 Wes. Law Monthly, 585.

b. To maintain replevin, the plaintiff must have a title that is recognized at law, and not merely a right that may be protected in equity. *Coe v. Peacock, et al.*, 2 Wes. Law Monthly, 133.

c. In replevin, the answer of *non detinet*, or that the defendant does not unlawfully detain the chattel claimed, puts in issue not only the fact of detention by the defendant, but also, that of the plaintiff's title to the chattel; and *non detinet* is the only proper answer in the action. Upon this issue, all rights of both parties may be shown and passed upon. *Kelley v. Blakeley*, 2 Wes. Law Monthly, 151.

d. In an action against a constable and his sureties on his official bond for taking the property of the plaintiff, under a writ of replevin against a third person, a verdict and judgment against the constable, in an action of trespass for taking the property, is admissible as *prima facie* evidence although the sureties had no notice of the action against the constable. *State of Ohio v. Colerick*, 3 Ohio, 487; and *Westerhaven v. Clive*, 5 Ohio, 136, followed; *State v. Jennings*, 14 O. St. 73.

e. In this state, a writ of replevin issues to enforce the claim of an owner of property, for its delivery to him by one who wrongfully detains it, and for that purpose the property is specifically described, but it confers no authority on the officer to seize property which is not actually or constructively in the possession of the party named in the process. And where the property is taken from the possession of a third person, who is the *bona fide* owner thereof, the process will not justify the officer, although it be the identical property described therein. *Ib*. 73.

f. It is the wrongful detention that gives the right of action in Ohio. Indeed, a *tortious taking*, without a subsequent *wrongful detention*, would not support the action. The writ can not issue without the affidavit of the party, or his agent or attorney, of such unlawful detention. Per Gholson, J.: *Ib*. 77.

g. When a constable makes a levy by virtue of an execution in his hands, upon property which is afterward replevied by a person claiming title to the property, by purchase from the judgment debtor prior to the levy, the plaintiff in replevin on executing an undertaking as required by law, acquires a right to the possession of the property, and all the interest in it which had been acquired by such constable by virtue of his levy; and this, notwithstanding it should be found that the alleged purchase by the plaintiff in replevin, was fraudulent and void as against creditors. *Crittenden v. Lingle*, 14 O. St 182.

h Such right of possession and interest is paramount to any right to be acquired by a subsequent levy on the same property under an execution against the same judgment debtor. *Ib*. 182.

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 211, 215, 221. 443

a. When such subsequent levy is made and the officer sells the property, such plaintiff in replevin may maintain an action of replevin against the purchaser. *Ib.* 182.

b. The "right and proper" damages given by the statute to the defendant, in an action of replevin brought by the mortgagee against the officer, when it appears that the mortgage lien upon the property exceeds its value, is not the value of such property, or the amount of the execution levied upon it, but nominal merely. *Coe v. Peacock*, 14 O. St. 187.

c. The interest of the mortgagor in chattel property mortgaged, of which he is in possession after condition broken, may be attached; and the levy of the order of attachment and seizure of the property, by the officer charged with the execution of the order, creates a lien in favor of the attaching creditor upon the interest of such mortgagor, and the subsequent recovery, by the mortgagee, of the property, in an action of replevin, from the officer holding it under the attachment, does not divest such lien; and the attaching creditor may subject the surplus of the proceeds remaining after satisfaction of the mortgage, to the payment of the judgment recovered in the attachment suit, although after the levy of the attachment, and before the creditor commenced proceedings to subject such surplus, the mortgagor had executed an assignment, to the mortgagee, of such surplus. *Carty v. Fenstemaker*, 14 O. St. 457.

### SECTION 211.

[Sec. 179, Ohio code, and 211, Kentucky code, are substantially alike.]

d. It is not necessary to the validity of an undertaking in replevin, that it should be signed by the principal. If signed by the surety, only, it is good; the judgment against the principal binding him. *Rhodenbaugh v. Carey*, 1 Wes. Law Monthly, 599.

e. Where an undertaking in replevin is for less than double the value of the appraised property, it is not therefore void. The defendant may waive such defect and accept the bond; and the obligors are bound thereby. It is no defense to such an undertaking for the surety and obligor, to show that the penalty is less than double the value of the aforesaid property under the 143d section of the justices' code. *Ib.*

### SECTION 215.

**BOND TO PERFORM JUDGMENT.**

f. *How bond construed.* The petition presented three causes of action, upon one of which plaintiff obtained an order for the delivery of personal property. The defendant thereupon executed bond *to perform the judgment of the court in the action*. Judgment was rendered upon all the causes of action. *Held*, that the bond only bound the obligors to perform the judgment on that cause of action upon which the order of delivery was sued out, and that the bond did not cover that part of the judgment based upon the other causes of action set out in the petition. *McKee v. Pope*, 18 B. Mon. 557.

### SECTION 221.

**ATTACHMENT—GENERAL PRINCIPLES.**

g. *Attachment laws to be strictly construed.* Attachments are extraordinary remedies, which may be employed greatly to the oppression and harassment of

446 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 221.

bailee of the goods attached, is not, of itself, proof of fraud charged against the attaching creditor, in attaching the debtor's goods to save them from other creditors. Stites, J.: Cannon v. Sorrell, Ms. Opin., October, 1856.

a. *Sale absolute: possession remaining with vendor fraudulent: vendor and vendee residing together.* If the possession of personal property remain with the vendor, the sale, if absolute, is deemed fraudulent. If the vendor be found in possession, after the sale, alleging that the purchaser took possession after the sale, and returned the property to him, such change of possession is insufficient, unless open, visible, and notorious. The same doctrine prevails where the vendor and vendee reside together, because residing at the same place, vendor is apparently in possession. Simpson, J.: Newland, Hughes, etc. v. Rutherford, Ms. Opin., June, 1856.

b. *Possession with vendor after sale, sale fraudulent.* The retention and use of personal property by the vendor, after a sale, renders such sale as to creditors and third persons, fraudulent and void (8 B. Mon. 16; 5 J. J. Marshall, 574, and cases there cited). Stites, J.: Oldham v. Tilton, Ms. Opin., December, 1856.

c. *Possession retained for a reasonable purpose.* The giving of power to trustees, to whom a debtor has assigned for the benefit of creditors, to finish any work on hand and fill any contract then already made, by a large manufacturing firm, is not regarded as sufficient to vitiate the deed, unless it is made to appear that such power was conferred with a fraudulent intent to hinder and delay creditors (2 B. Mon. 358). Duvall, J.: Belknap v. Olmstead, etc., Ms. Opin., January, 1856.

d. *Affidavit that intent was to hinder and delay, without stating that it was fraudulent.* Where the affidavit on which an attachment issues, only states that the conveyance by defendant, of his property, was to hinder, delay, etc., his creditors, and fails to state that the *intent was fraudulent*, the attachment should be discharged. Marshall, C.J.: Merrill v. Bridgewater Ms. Opin., June, 1855.

e. *There must be an intent to delay.* The doctrine is well settled, that the mere fact that a conveyance will have the effect of hindering and delaying creditors, will not render it fraudulent. Every conveyance of any part of the debtor's property, may have that effect, in a greater or less degree. There must be an *intent and purpose to delay*, and the deed must be the means to delay or defraud (4 B. Mon. 430); and the trustee must accept it with like intent and purpose. Duvall, J.: Hart v. Yantes, Ms. Opin., June, 1857.

f. *Parent indebted at time of gift to child.* The mere circumstance that a parent is indebted at the time he makes an advancement to his child, does not, of itself, render the gift void; it is the *intent to defraud creditors* that vitiates such a gift (3 Marshall, 239). Simpson, J.: Mosely v. Taylor, Ms. Opin., October, 1856.

g. *Conveyance in consideration of future service.* A debtor has no right to convey his property to another, in consideration of his future services, as it is, in effect conveying it for his own benefit, and to the prejudice of his creditors. *Ib.*

h. *Vendor to remain in possession.* A deed of assignment to a trustee, which provides on its face that the assignor is to remain in possession of his property, for the purpose of carrying on business, and is to lay out the receipts in stock

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 221. 447

and pay the profits to his creditors, after paying himself for his services, is, *per se*, fraudulent; nor is it made otherwise by the trustee taking possession of the property, in contravention of its terms. Simpson, J.: Rice *v.* Bodecker, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

*a.* *Is about to waste or dispose of his property with a fraudulent intent, etc., sufficient.* The affidavit states the nature of the claim, that it is just, and the amount the affiant believes he is entitled to recover. It also states, that the defendant "is about to waste or dispose of his property, with the fraudulent intent to cheat or delay his creditors in the collecting of their debts." This is substantially the ground mentioned in the 8th subdivision of the civil code upon which an attachment may issue. Lane, etc. *v.* Robinson, 18 B. Mon. 632.

*b.* *Voluntary conveyance of wife's earnings, fraudulent as to creditors.* No principle is better settled than that the proceeds of the labor and industry of the wife, during the coverture, rest absolutely in the husband and become part of his estate, and subject to the payment of his debts. No voluntary disposition of such property, by him, or her with his consent, can operate to the prejudice of his then existing creditors. 7 B. Mon. 444; Stites, J.: Records *v.* Gatewood, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

*c.* *Assignment for benefit of creditors not per se fraudulent.* It is not *per se*, fraudulent or unlawful, for a debtor to assign for the benefit of all his creditors, after having been sued by some. Wheat, C. J.: Gordon, etc. *v.* Jackson, etc., Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

*d.* *Grantee must be shown to have participated in fraud.* The actual participation of the grantee, or beneficiary, in a deed of trust for the benefit of creditors, in the fraudulent motive of the grantor, must be proved, before the transaction can be vacated apon the ground of fraud. 3 B. Mon. 557; 7 B. Mon. 359; Duval, J.: Cromie *v.* Grainger, Ms. Opin, September, 1857.

*e.* *Creditor participating in fraud, allowed to retain debt.* A *bona fide* creditor, who obtains possession of his debtor's goods, under a sale adjudged fraudulent, and in which fraud he, the creditor, participated, will be allowed out of the proceeds of the sale of the goods, enough to satisfy his demand. Planters' Bank of Tennessee *v.* Baker, etc.. Ms. Opin., Fall Term, 1854. Wheat, J.: Simon, etc. *v.* Ball, Ms. Opin., September, 1857.

[Sec. 191, Ohio code, prescribing grounds of attachment, is substantially the same as 221, Kentucky code.]

*f.* Where one partner absconds, and the other disposes of a part, and is disposing of the whole partnership effects, it will be presumed that they intend to hinder and delay their creditors, so as to form good ground for attachment against the partnership property. Sellew *v.* Chrisfield & Peale, 1 Handy, 86.

*g.* Where one partner avoids service of process by absconding, and the other copartner remaining in possession of the stock of goods is insolvent, an attachment may be laid on the partnership property. *Ib.*

*h.* *Semble*, that the absconding of one partner, would be a good cause for an attachment against partnership property. *Ib.*

*i.* One partner may assign all of the copartnership property, without the presence or assent of his copartner, if done in good faith. *Ib.*

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 222. 449

supplying the place of a separate affidavit. (Scott v. Doneghy, 17 B. Mon. 324.) Franklin Sav. Ins. v. Bank of Wheeling, 1 Met. 156; *Ib.* 470.

a. *Same.* An affidavit to obtain an attachment under the civil code (secs. 221, 222), must state, in terms or in substance, the nature of the plaintiff's claim, that the claim is just, and the amount which the plaintiff believes he is entitled to recover. These provisions of the code must be strictly complied with. It will, however, be sufficient if the requisite averments are contained in the petition, and it is sworn to, as decided in Scott v. Doneghy, 17 B. Mon. 324. Worthington v. Cary, etc., 1 Met. 470.

b. *Affiant must state that he believes the debt just.* An affidavit should state that the affiant believes the debt sued for, and for which attachment is sought, is just. *Quere*, could the affidavit be amended after the attachment is levied? Taylor v. Smith, 17 B. Mon. 542.

c. *Otherwise affidavit insufficient.* Subsections 2 and 3. Although an affidavit for an attachment states that the plaintiff believes he will recover the amount of his claim, if it fails to state that the claim is just, and that he ought to recover the amount, it is defective. Crenshaw, C. J.: Samuel v. Dallam, Ms. Opin., January, 1857.

d. *Statement of several grounds in the alternative, sufficient.* Although an affidavit presents several states of case in the alternative, either one of which would be sufficient to sustain an attachment, yet, as it was in the language of the code itself, and is an exact copy of one of the grounds of attachment in sec. 221, it must be deemed sufficient. Simpson, J.: Harvod v. Orear, Ms. Opin., December, 1856.

e. *Same.* Such statements of the various modes of the disposition of defendant's property can not take him by surprise, as he must know in which of the modes he has disposed of his property. *Ib.*

f. *That defendant "will remove," not equivalent to saying he is "about to remove."* Subsection 4. An affidavit for an attachment, in attempting to set forth the grounds of attachment contained in subsection 6, of sec. 220, stated that "the plaintiff verily believes the said defendant will fraudulently sell and remove his property from the commonwealth"—*Held*, defective, because the expression "will," etc., is not equivalent to "about," etc.; nor does the statement negative the idea that the defendant might leave property enough in the state to satisfy plaintiff's claim. *Ib.*

g. *Substantial statement sufficient.* It is not requisite in the affidavit to state the grounds of the attachment in the very words of the code, but words equivalent thereto are required. *Ib.*

h. *Admission by defendant that claim is just, does not cure defect.* The admission of defendant, in his answer, that the plaintiff's claim is just, does not cure the defect arising from a failure of a plaintiff to state in his affidavit that the claim is just, where the affidavit has been made the foundation of a judgment against garnishees. *Quere*, against the defendant alone? *Ib.*

i. *That the affiant "believes" the facts, etc.* The affidavit stated that "the affiant believed that defendant, R., has left C. county, the county of his residence, to avoid the service of legal process. He believes that R. so conceals himself that a summons can not be served on him," which statement was sworn to as true, and the defendant responded to it without objection to its sufficiency.

classification.title: Bond Liability of Obligor Upon Attachment
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 details the liability of an obligor on an attachment bond. It clarifies what damages are recoverable, distinguishing between costs of the attachment itself and costs of the suit. It also explains the difference between an action on the bond and an action for malicious prosecution, emphasizing that the former depends on whether the attachment was rightfully or wrongfully obtained, while the latter requires proof of malice and want of probable cause. The discharge of an attachment is considered conclusive evidence that it was obtained without good cause.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 discusses legal principles related to liens and priorities, particularly in the context of attachments and sales. It covers scenarios such as the order of attachment, fraudulent levies, the timing of lien creation, sales in contemplation of insolvency, and the attachment of assigned debts or steamboats. The text focuses on the legal mechanics of establishing and enforcing financial claims against property and debts.

classification.title: Forthcoming Bond
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 details the legal requirements and effects of forthcoming bonds in civil cases, particularly in relation to attachments. It clarifies that informal bonds can be valid as common-law obligations, outlines the purpose of a forthcoming bond (ensuring property is available for judgment or payment of damages), and distinguishes it from a bond that discharges an attachment. It also addresses situations where a wrong bond is executed and the party is still bound.

entries.ocr_text:
CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 239, 242. 455

[Sec. 199, Ohio code, and 235, Kentucky code, are the same.]

a. The interest of a partner in partnership property may be attached; and the sheriff is entitled to the exclusive possession of the property attached, until he sells the interest attached. *Stewart & Co. v. Hunter & Moore*, 1 Handy, 22.

b. It is the *property of the defendant* which is to be appraised, and where such property extends not to the whole of any articles or chattels, but to a lesser interest, it is that interest determined in each case by the number of the partners, which is to be appraised. *Ib.*

c. Where the interest of the defendant in the property attached is less than the whole, the penalty of the bond will be double the appraised value of such interest, and not double the value of the property. *Stewart & Co. v. Hunter & Moore*, 1 Handy, 22.

d. An undertaking on the part of a defendant, given to release property from an attachment levied by a sheriff or officer, is valid as a common law security, though no appraisement is made, and the security is approved by the sheriff, instead of the court, during the term of the court in which the attachment is returnable. *Sheldon v. Sharpless*, 1 Wes. Law Monthly, 42.

e. The interest of a mortgagor in chattel property mortgaged of which he is in possession after condition broken, may be attached; and the levy of the order of attachment and seizure of the property, by the officer charged with the execution of the order, creates a lien in favor of the attaching creditor upon the interest of such mortgagor. *Carty v. Fenstemaker*, 14 O. St. 457.

### SECTION 239.
### DISPOSITION OF ATTACHED PROPERTY.

f. *Court may order sale of perishable property under act of 1856.* The circuit court has the right, in a proceeding under the statute of 1856, to order a sale of perishable property embraced in the mortgage, in the absence of any allegation in plaintiff's petition that the same would be illegally disposed of, or would not be forthcoming at the rendition of the judgment. *Act of 1856, supra, sec. 4*; Civil Code, sec. 239; *Terrill v. Jennings*, 1 Met. 450.

g. *Sale not complete till confirmed by court.* A sale of attached property, made by the sheriff, under an order issued in vacation by the presiding judge of the county court (Civil Code, sec. 239), is not final and complete without a report by the officer, and its confirmation by the circuit court in which the attachment is pending, which has jurisdiction to confirm or set aside sales so made. *Greer, etc. v. Powell, etc.*, 1 Met. 124.

h. *Upon what proof sale may be ordered.* Under sec. 239, the court may order the sale of attached personal property upon parol proof, and the correctness of the order will be presumed, nothing to the contrary appearing. *Dunn v. Salter*, 1 Duv. 342.

### SECTION 242.
### BOND DISCHARGING ATTACHMENT.

i. *Bond prescribed by sec. 242, admits the grounds on which attachment is obtained.* The execution of the bond for the retention of attached property as authorized by secs. 242 and 243 of the code, operates to discharge the attachment, and the obligors in the bond must perform the judgment of the court. classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Civil Code of Practice details rules and procedures related to attachments and garnishment in civil legal actions. It clarifies the conditions under which bonds are executed for retaining property, discharging attachments, and the process for handling garnishees who are found to be indebted to a defendant. It specifies that while a garnishee can be ordered to pay into court, a personal judgment against them is not permissible without a separate action.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 details procedures related to garnishees in civil attachments. It outlines how a garnishee's debt can be secured and applied to a judgment, the court's control over debts not yet due, the prohibition of personal judgments against a mere garnishee, the liability of a garnishee who neglects to defend, and the garnishee's entitlement to counsel fees. It also touches upon the sale of property of absent defendants, requiring an affidavit that the defendant has no personal property before real property can be sold.

462 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 297.

**a. *Effect of injunction not changed by code*.** The civil code has not diminished or changed the force and effect either of an injunction or a supersedeas. The mode of procuring such process has been changed, but when procured it is entitled to the same force it always had. Keith v. Wilson, 3 Met. 201.

DISSOLUTION—REINSTATEMENT—EFFECT—REMEDY.

**b. *Execution enjoined, releases levy*.** Where the execution of a *fieri facias*, which has been levied, is arrested by either an injunction or supersedeas, the levy is released, and the lien created thereby discharged. The officer has no lawful control over the property after the service of either precept, and it becomes then his duty, if it is in his possession, to restore it to the defendant. 3 Met. 201.

**c. *Injunction dissolved—venditioni can not issue*.** Where the execution of a *fieri facias*, which has been levied, has been stopped by injunction or supersedeas, upon discharge thereof, a writ of *venditioni exponas* can not issue, and if issued it will be void, and, together with the sale made thereunder, will be quashed. *Ib*.

**d. *Remedy on injunction bond*.** In such case, the plaintiff in the *fieri facias* has his recourse upon the injunction and appeal bonds for any damage sustained by the effect of the injunction and supersedeas, and the purchaser of the property under the writ of *venditioni exponas* is not without his remedy. *Argu*. *Ib*.

**e. *No action on bond till case finally disposed of*.** After answer to an action in equity, brought for a settlement of a partnership, an injunction and restraining order, obtained by the plaintiff, was, on motion of the defendant, discharged, but no order made finally disposing of the action. The defendant in said action thereupon sued to recover damages against the plaintiff for maliciously, and without probable cause, bringing said action and suing out said injunction—*Held*, that the order discharging the injunction was interlocutory, and there not having been a final determination of the suit, the action for malicious prosecution can not be maintained. Wood v. Laycock, 3 Met. 192.

**f. *Mode of estimating damage*.** It is error to assess damages on the amount of the judgment enjoined and entered up to the dissolution. It should be estimated on the judgment and interest up to the time of granting the injunction. Marshall, J.: Austin v. Redenbrou, Ms. Opin., December, 1854.

**g. *Sec. 325 no application to attachments*.** Sec. 325 Civil Code has no application to attachments sued out by creditors against a debtor. Upon the discharge of an attachment obtained to subject *choses in action* to the satisfaction of a debt, the actual damages sustained by reason of the suing out of the attachment, is the measure of damages. 18 B. Mon. 52; Stites, J.: Shelby v. Alber's ex'rs, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

**h. *Failing to set up usury at law—can not enjoin and how it allowed*.** Usury in the debt sought to be collected was known to the party before the judgment by ordinary proceedings was rendered against him, and was then available as a defense either at law or in equity. He failed to rely on it, however, but seeks an injunction or modification of the judgment to the extent of the usury. *Held*, that he is not entitled to the relief. Civil Code, sec. 14; Greer v. George, Ms Opin., December, 1856; Chinn v. Mitchell, 2 Met. 92.

**i. *Injunction dissolved before case finally decided, is not a final order*.** Vendee

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases - 1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses the use of injunctions in civil litigation. It details various scenarios where injunctions can be granted, such as preventing a multiplicity of suits, enjoining parties from suing in foreign tribunals, or preventing proceedings on judgments obtained without jurisdiction. It also clarifies jurisdictional limitations, such as a state court's inability to enjoin a federal court judgment and vice versa. Specific examples of injunctions include restraining the transfer of stock, aiding in the recovery of personal property, preventing obstruction of watercourses, enforcing lease terms, restraining the publication of private letters, preventing misappropriation of funds, restraining nuisances, and preventing waste or injury to land. One point (l) mentions restraining proceedings under an act to abolish imprisonment for debt.

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 342, 343, 345. 473

court having control of the fund, furnishes no protection to the party making it. Chinn v. Mitchell, 2 Met. 92.

SECTIONS 342, 343.

TRIAL, BY COURT OR JURY—WAIVER.

a. *Failing to appear, waiver of jury.* The failing to appear to a suit by ordinary petition is a waiver of the right of trial by jury. Daniel v. Judy, 14 B. Mon. 395.

b. *Transfer to equity, court to try issue.* The issues of fact arising in a proceeding by an ordinary proceeding for the recovery of money, or of real or personal property, are to be tried by jury, unless it be waived. By an agreement to transfer a case to the equity docket, all the issues, whether legal or equitable, in the case are to be tried as equitable issues are tried. Smith v. Moberly, 15 B. Mon. 74.

c. *Issue in equity proper for jury—jury waived unless motion to transfer made.* When the petition is in equity, and an issue is made proper for the decision of a jury, the defendant may ask that the case be transferred to the ordinary docket, as prescribed by the civil code. Where no such motion is made, and the cause is submitted for trial, without objection from either side, the error as to the form of the proceedings is waived, and it is the duty of the court to decide the cause, or call a jury to decide the issue. It is error to dismiss the petition without prejudice. Frazer, etc. v. Naylor, etc., 1 Met. 593.

d. *Order to try by jury, sufficiency of.* Where, in an action pending in equity, the only issue made by the pleadings was as to the liability of the defendant to the plaintiff for the demand asserted by the latter, and the extent of such liability, if it existed, which was transferred to the common-law docket to be tried by a jury, the order on the subject to the effect that the issue raised by the pleadings was to be tried by a jury, is sufficiently explicit. Saving's Bank v. Benton, 2 Met. 240.

e. *Jury trial of issue out of equity, oral evidence competent.* Upon the trial by a jury of an issue out of equity, oral testimony is always admissible. Depositions may be used, unless the court otherwise directs, in which case, oral testimony alone will be admissible. (Civil Code, sec. 613.) *Ib.*

f. *Several paragraphs in answer—trial, appeal and decision against plaintiff on one—may afterward have trial on the others.* An answer contained three paragraphs. Plaintiffs demurred to each, and demurrer overruled, to which they except, but elect to stand upon demurrer to first paragraph, and thereupon judgment goes against them in the action. Upon appeal—*Held*, that the demurrer to the first paragraph ought to have been sustained, and judgment therefore reversed. Upon a return of the case, the plaintiffs would be entitled to a trial on the second and third paragraphs, they presenting affirmative matter of defense to which no reply was necessary. Whitehead v. Root, 2 Met. 584.

SECTION 345.

CONTINUANCE

g. *Continuance after trial commenced.* Continuance may be granted after the trial has commenced upon the ground of surprise, if the court believes the ends of justice require it. Simpson, J.: Davis v. Brown, Ms. Opin., September, 1853.

h. *Absence of principal counsel.* Unavoidable absence of principal counsel

474 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 347.

may be a sufficient cause for a continuance, but no valid ground for a new trial, unless it so prejudiced the case of the party applying, that, upon another trial, a different result might be produced. Simpson, J.: Shelby R. R. Co. v. Pearce, etc., Ms. Opin., June, 1855.

a. *Sufficiency of affidavit.* An affidavit for continuance is defective when it fails to state that the party *believes he can prove facts* which he is informed certain witnesses know. Simpson, J.: Smith v. Haley, Ms. Opin., Dec. 1856.

b. *Same. Must show materiality of evidence, and due diligence.* An affidavit for a continuance must not only show the materiality of the evidence expected to be obtained, but must show that due diligence was used to obtain it. Depositions can not be taken until answer is filed (sec. 618), therefore, it is not diligence to attempt to take a deposition before the filing of the answer. So, efforts to take upon interrogatories without leave of the court (sec. 637). The affidavit, in addition to the averment of the materiality of the evidence, and of due diligence, must aver that affiant believes the facts he expects to prove to be true. Stites, J.: Waller v. Eddy, Ms. Opin., June, 1857.

c. *Same. And must state that affiant believes the facts are true.* An affidavit for a continuance must not only state what facts the affiant expects to prove by the absent witness, but must also state that he believes these facts to be true. Simpson, J.: Haynes, etc. v. Hawkins, Ms. Opin., February, 1858.

d. *Upon affirmance and return of mandate, new trial can not be had.* When the mandate of the court of appeals was entered in the circuit court, the defendant moved for a continuance, upon affidavits filed, in order that he might have time to procure additional testimony, which he alleged he had discovered since the former trial. The application for a continuance was overruled, and—*Held*, that the mandate was imperative, and must be entered. McLean v. Nixon, 18 B. Mon. 774.

e. *Witness out of county, party must tender fees and expenses before continuance.* A continuance ought not to be allowed a party on the ground of the absence of witnesses residing out of the county, unless he shows that he has made the requisite tender or payment of the witnesses' traveling expenses going to and returning from court, or that the witness has waived the same. Thurman v. Virgin and wife, 18 B. Mon. 790.

SECTION 347.
BURDEN OF PROOF.

f. *Confession and avoidance, defendant entitled to conclusion.* The plaintiff's petition alleges trespass in tearing down fences, etc. The defendant's answer alleges an agreement between plaintiff and himself, that either should separate his fence from the other where they joined, when he chose so to do—*Held*, that the defendant was entitled to the affirmative. Marshall, C. J.: Beckley v. Brown, Ms. Opin., December, 1855.

g. *And that, though plaintiff first introduced his proof.* In such case, though the plaintiff first introduced his proof as to the trespass, etc., yet, as the defendant introduced proof in justification, he had a right to the concluding argument. *Ib.*

h. *Same. How defense construed.* The license pleaded in such case, must be understood as an admission that the possession of the *locus in quo* was in the plaintiff. *Ib.*

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 347. 475

a. *The burden is with the propounders of a will.* The *onus probandi* lies with the propounders of the will in the circuit court. Upon a writ of error from an order of the county court establishing the same will, they have the concluding of the argument. (1 B. Mon. 339.) Stites, J.: Jones, etc. v. Greene, etc., Ms. Opin., January, 1857.

b. *Defendant denies plaintiffs' title, and avers that title is in his wife, defendant entitled to conclusion.* Plaintiffs alleged in their petition that they were in possession of the slave in contest, of which they were the owners, and that she had been unlawfully taken from their possession by the defendant. The latter admitted in his answer that the plaintiffs had the possession of the slave, and that he had taken her from their possession; but denied that they were the owners of her, and alleged that she belonged to his wife—*Held*, that the burden of proof was upon the defendant, and he had a right to the conclusion of the argument to the jury. Vance v. Vance, 2 Met. 581.

c. *Defendant having burden of proof, entitled to conclude.* Where the burden of proof is on the defendant, he has the right to the conclusion of the argument before the jury. Tipton v. Triplett, 1 Met. 570.

ISSUE—INSTRUCTIONS.

d. *Failure to reduce instruction to writing when required, error.* The failure of the court to reduce its instructions to writing, when required by either party, as provided by *Civil Code*, sec. 347, is error sufficient to reverse the judgment. Ferguson v. Fox's adm'r, 83.

e. *Same.* Either party may require the instructions of the court to be reduced to writing before the jury retire to consult about their verdict; but the requisition should be made in such a manner as not to mislead the court, but to inform it clearly of the desire of the party on the subject. *Ib.*

f. *But counsel must except at proper time or error waived.* After the trial had ended, "one of the jury asked the court if the instructions were in writing, to which the court replied they were not, but the jury could retire and the instructions would be reduced to writing, and if they had any difficulty they could send for the written instructions. The counsel for the plaintiff then said he hoped the instructions would be reduced to writing." *Held*, that the language used was equivocal and not sufficient to apprise the court that the counsel required the instructions to be reduced to writing. The requisition should be made in such a manner as not to mislead the court, but to inform it clearly of the desire of the party on the subject. *Ib.*

g. *Instructions leaving jury to determine what facts in pleadings are in issue, erroneous.* Whether a fact, alleged in the pleadings, be material or immaterial to the matter in issue, and whether it has been denied in the answer or the response, are questions of law which the court should determine, and inform the jury what facts are to be considered by them as true under the pleadings. An instruction that "every fact material to the matters in issue in this suit which is stated in defendant's answer, so far as it sets up a counterclaim against plaintiff, and which is not denied by plaintiff's answer thereto, the jury are bound to receive as true, is improper and erroneous" Tipton v. Triplett, 1 Met. 570.

h. *Same.* An instruction to the jury that every material allegation in plaintiff's petition, not denied by the answer, must, for the purposes of the action, be
