CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 350. 477

consider the testimony of *any witness who knows* the reputation of the impeached witness among those with whom he dwells or is conversant. *Ib.*

**i. Slander—evidence competent to show malice but not to increase damage—caution required in instructing jury.** Y. and wife brought an action of slander against L. and wife for slanderous words alleged to have been spoken by Mrs. L. concerning Mrs. Y. The plaintiffs introduced evidence conducing to prove the speaking of the words by Mrs. L., and that they were similar in substance to those contained in an anonymous letter said to have been published by her, and that she had caused it to be written and sent to Mrs. Y. Plaintiffs, after introducing evidence conducing to show its publication, offered the letter in evidence, to which defendants objected. The letter contained libelous imputations against both Mrs. Y. and her daughter. *Held*—That the letter was admissible to show malice against Mrs. Y., but not for any other purpose; that the court should have cautioned the jury that they were not to increase the damages on account of the letter, or give it any effect whatever in estimating the amount of damages, which caution should accompany its admission; and that so much of it as related to the daughter of the plaintiff could not be considered as evidence against the defendants for any purpose. *Letton v. Young, 2 Met. 558.*

**a. Same. Instructions held indefinite and misleading.** In such case an instruction was given to the jury to the effect that the letter in question was only admissible to show malice, and for no other purpose. And in another instruction the jury were told that they had a right to award such damages to the plaintiffs as they thought them entitled to from *all the facts and circumstances* proved in the case. *Held*, that the caution to the jury in respect to the effect to which the letter was entitled was not sufficient. *Ib.*

**b. Same. Court must define to jury what words are slanderous.** An instruction in an action of slander hypothecated upon the belief of the jury "that the slanderous words set forth in the petition, or any part of them," were spoken but which fails to inform the jury what words set out in the petition were slanderous and actionable, is misleading, especially if the petition contain expressions charged to have been spoken which are not slanderous. *Ib.*

**c. Jury not bound to disregard all witness says who may have testified falsely.** It is error to instruct the jury "that if they believe any witness has sworn falsely and knowingly as to any material fact, they are *bound to disregard his testimony altogether*." Whenever a person is held competent, and allowed to testify as a witness, the jury are the exclusive judges of his credibility, and may give such weight to his evidence as they may, under the circumstances, think it deserves. 1 Starkie, 454; 1 Greenleaf, 380; *Ib.*

**d. Instructions must be excepted to, or error waived.** Errors of the court in granting or refusing instructions, unless excepted to at the time, are not only unavailing for a new trial below, but are likewise unavailing for reversal in the court of appeals. *Ib.*

**e. Abstract instructions erroneous.** An instruction unsupported by the evidence is abstract and erroneous. *Revill v. Petitt, 3 Met. 314.*

**f. Where court may instruct peremptorily.** Where the evidence sustaining plaintiff's claim was uncontradicted, it was not error to instruct the jury to find for the plaintiff if they believed the testimony. *Spalding v. Bull, 1 Duv. 311.*

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 357, 359, 360. 479

the jury have a right, in the absence of all proof of the value of such labor, and from their own knowledge of the ordinary transactions and business of society, and especially from their presumed knowledge of the value of labor, to find a verdict for the price of the work done and for which the action was brought (1 J. J. Mar. 336). The principle applies to judges as well as jurors. Where a jury is waived, and the law and facts are submitted to the court, the order of submission clothes the court with all the rights and functions of a jury in determining the facts and in the assessment of damages. *Baum v. Winston*, 3 Met. 127.

a. Upon the trial of an issue of fact, a special verdict should find facts to which the law gives a determinate effect, conclusive of the issue. No judgment can be rendered upon a finding of facts which are in the nature of evidence only, and are not, *in law*, conclusive upon the question at issue. *Leech v. Kagy's adm'r*, 9 O. St.

SECTION 357.

b. *Special verdict.* Where legal as well as equitable matters of defense arise upon issues commenced at law, the legal issues should be tried by a jury, and the court may submit matters of fact arising upon the equitable issues to the jury, where such issues have not been transferred to the equitable docket. In such cases the finding of the jury should be special to enable the court to decide the case. *Petty v. Malier*, 15 B. Mon. 604.

SECTION 359.

c. Sec. 359 relates only to cases involving questions of value and damage, as questions of fact. As a general rule the jury must in such cases assess the amount of recovery. *Cooper v. Paston*, 1 Duv. 92.

SECTION 360.

d. *Must be same evidence to authorize assessment of damage.* To authorize a jury to assess damages for property illegally taken and detained (hogs), there must be some evidence of the value, or some description to enable the jury to determine the value from their own knowledge. *Pharis, etc. v. Carver*, 13 B. Mon. 238.

e. *Jury must fix value of each article.* Under this section the jury should fix, in their verdict, the value of each article of property found to belong to the plaintiff, and the judgment should conform to the verdict. Marshall, C. J.: *Graves v. Watkins*, Ms. Opin., June, 1856.

f. *Same.* A verdict for $100 in damages, does not authorize a judgment for the return of specific property, and in cases like this, it is obvious how necessary it is to conform to the requisitions of sec. 360. Stites, J.: *Barrett v. Crabtree*, Ms. Opin., June, 1856.

g. *Judgment erroneous if jury fail to assess value of each article.* In an action to recover the possession of specific personal property, the plaintiff, upon filing the affidavit required by sec. 208 of Civil Code, and executing bond, obtained possession thereof. His right to the property was controverted by the defendants, and upon the trial the jury returned this verdict: "We of the jury find for the defendants." Thereupon the court rendered judgment for a return of the property, or for $90, being the value of the property as proved upon the trial. *Held*

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 364. 481

to the adoption of the code (3 Marshall, 360). Duvall, J.: Reuben, etc. v. Robinson, etc., Ms. Opin., June, 1857.

a. *Time can not be extended beyond succeeding term.* The court has no power to extend the time for reducing exceptions to writing beyond the succeeding term after trial and judgment. Simpson, J.: Porter v. January, Ms. Opin., July, 1856.

b. *But criminal or equity term intervening, not regarded.* Where an order gives time to prepare and file a bill of exceptions, in an action at law, until the succeeding term, it will not be construed to refer to a criminal or chancery term, which, in some districts, may intervene. Wheat, C. J.: Wright v. Watrous, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

c. *Objection because bill not filed in time, can not be made for first time in court of appeals.* A bill of exceptions for which a certain day has been given to prepare and file, but which was not prepared or filed on that day, and was afterward filed without objection, an objection made here for the first time can not avail the party complaining (Singleton's adm'rs v. Kennedy, etc., 9 B. Mon. 223). The instruction made to the contrary in Freeman v. Brenham, 17 B. Mon. 609, can not be regarded as authority. Duvall, J.: Wood v. Forman, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

d. *Action of circuit court presumed correct unless contrary shown.* The court of appeals, in the absence of a bill of exceptions, containing the evidence and instructions to the jury, will presume that the action of the circuit court was correct, and will not disturb the verdict. Brandis v. Stewart, 1 Met. 395.

e. *Errors not excepted to, waived.* A decision of the circuit court excluding testimony, and not excepted to, can not be inquired into by the court of appeals (Civil Code, sec. 653.) Terrill v. Jennings, 1 Met. 450.

f. *Code has not changed former law.* The rule of practice which required bills of exceptions to be filed in term time has not been changed by the code. Allard v. Smith, 2 Met. 297.

g. *Bill can not be filed in vacation.* An order was made allowing forty days to prepare a bill of exceptions containing a statement of the evidence, to which both parties excepted. The bill was made out, signed by the judge and filed, in vacation, the clerk certifying that *the parties filed it* in his office—*Held*, that it forms no part of the record. *Ib.*

h. *Rule as to time, to be strictly adhered to.* Time may be given to reduce the exception to writing, but not beyond the succeeding term (Civil Code, sec. 364). This rule, as expounded in the case of Freeman v. Brenham (17 B. Mon. 607), and in subsequent decisions, should be strictly adhered to. Tweed v. Commonwealth, 2 Met. 378.

i. *Bill filed after succeeding term unavailing.* Judgment rendered 2d March, 1859. On the 5th of the same month, time was allowed "until the next June term of this court to file bill of exceptions herein." June 13, 1859, defendant "filed a bill of exceptions herein which is noted, and moved the court to make said bill, *when signed*, a part of the record in this case;" but it had not been allowed or certified or signed as a bill of exceptions by the judge. After the expiration of the June term—on the 18th of August—a bill of exceptions was filed, the order reciting, "the parties produced a bill of exceptions herein, *which was signed and noted*, and ordered to be made part of the record herein." *Held*, that

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases provides notes and case law interpretations related to Section 365, focusing on the requirements and amendments of bills of exceptions in legal proceedings. It details how records, papers, and testimony must be presented and included in bills of exceptions for them to be considered part of the official record and for appellate review. The notes cover scenarios involving the admissibility of records, amendments to bills of exceptions, the insertion of letters or papers, the finality of signed bills of exceptions, and the proper statement of objections and proposed evidence.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the 'Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases' from 1867. The content discusses procedural rules for legal cases, specifically concerning 'bills of exceptions.' These are legal documents used to record objections and rulings made during a trial so that they can be reviewed by a higher court. The text details how these bills should be prepared, amended, and what they must contain to be valid. CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 367, 368, 369. 487

**a. Papers copied in record, but not made part of it.** Title papers copied into the records, but not made part of the bill of exceptions, can not be regarded by the court of appeals as forming any part of the evidence that was heard upon the trial of the cause. Vaughn v. Mills, 18 B. Mon. 634.

SECTION 367.

**b. How bill of exceptions to be certified when judge did not preside at trial.** Where the judge who presided at the trial of the case did not preside when the motion for a new trial was overruled, the bill of exceptions must be certified by the affidavits of persons present at the trial, and who recollect it sufficiently well to make affidavit thereto. Simpson, J.: Cambron v. Caughtry, Ms. Opin., February, 1856.

SECTION 368.

**c. Jury not sworn.** That the jury was sworn in the circuit court, "to try the issue," when there was no issue, is an exception (when taken in this court for the first time) purely technical, and will not reverse. Marshall, J.: Swaringen v. Hubbard, Ms. Opin., September, 1854.

**d. Exceptions undisposed of by lower court.** A failure to dispose of exceptions to a commissioner's report, before decree, will not reverse it, if it appear that the error worked no injustice to the party excepting. Wheat, C. J.: Branham v. Rodgers; Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

SECTION 369.

**NEW TRIAL—IRREGULARITY—MISCONDUCT.**

**e. Assessing damages by dividing aggregate estimate of all the jurors by twelve.** For jurors to make verdicts by each putting down the respective amounts they thought defendant ought to pay (in actions sounding in damages), and dividing the aggregate by twelve, and basing their verdict on the result, in the absence of fraud, is no misconduct, and was expressly sanctioned by this court in 1 Bibb, 389; Hardin, 586; McCoy v. Vangeison, Ms. Opin., December, 1855.

**f. Same. Improper to instruct jury that they have right to do so.** In an action to recover damages for an alleged breach of an agreement to marry, an instruction that, if the jury believed that the plaintiff ought to recover, but disagreed as to the amount, "they have the right, each one, to set down the sum he believes ought to be recovered by the plaintiff, and add them all together, then divide the sum total by the number twelve, and find that amount, twelfth part, in damages, if they think proper to do so," is erroneous. Allard v. Smith, 1 Met. 297.

**g. That jury, after retiring, calls witness to their room, and examines him, is misconduct.** After the jury had retired, one of the witnesses who had been examined on the trial for the appellant, was sent for by them, admitted into the jury room, and re-examined, without the knowledge or consent of the court or the parties—*Held*, that, although he was the witness of the party seeking to reverse the judgment, and testified only to the same matters in reference to which he had testified on the trial, it was misconduct in the jury, and a new trial for this reason ought to have been granted. Lutrell v. Maysville & Lexington R. R. Co., 18 B. Mon. 294.

**h. Same. New trial should be granted.** The safest rule to adopt, in such

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases provides notes and interpretations related to Sections 367, 368, and 369. It covers procedural aspects of legal practice, including how bills of exceptions are certified, the implications of a jury not being sworn, the handling of undisposed exceptions to a commissioner's report, and rules regarding jury misconduct in assessing damages. Specifically, it clarifies that jurors averaging damages is not misconduct unless fraud is involved, but instructing them they have the right to do so is erroneous. It also details that a jury re-examining a witness in their room without consent is grounds for a new trial.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Civil Code of Practice provides notes and annotations related to sections 370 and 371, focusing on the grounds and procedures for granting a new trial in civil cases. It discusses the court's consideration of evidence competency and effect, the requirement for newly discovered evidence to necessitate a different verdict, and the denial of new trials for cumulative or impeaching evidence. It also addresses new trials for inadequate damages in tort actions, the court's power to set aside verdicts, and the influence of improperly admitted depositions on jury deliberations. Finally, it outlines the timeframe for applying for a new trial.

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 374. 495

time it should have been delivered, could be recovered upon a common count for use and occupation. Butler and Lane v. Baker, 5 O. St. 584.

a. In an action of trespass for an injury to a building occupied by the plaintiff as a store, resulting in an interruption of his business, he may recover, in addition to the damages done to the building, such further sum as will compensate him for the loss of its enjoyment while such interruption continued. For this purpose it is competent to prove the nature and extent of the business, the necessity of using the building for its prosecution, and the value of such use to him during the period of interruption. But in the absence of fraud or malice, or other circumstances justifying the recovery of exemplary damages, the amount of profits which might have been realized by employing his personal services and capital, in the prosecution of his business in the injured building during such interruption, can not be recovered. In such case the loss of profits does not furnish a proper rule for estimating the damages; but the loss of the use of the property, and the value of such use to the injured party, is all that can be recovered. City of Cincinnati v. Evans, 5 O. St. 594.

b. An action for a nuisance caused by any obstruction or diversion, of the water of a stream for any domestic, agricultural, and manufacturing purpose, will not lie, unless the damage occasioned thereby be real, material, and substantial. McElroy v. Goble, 6 O. St. 187.

c. Where parties entered a house to search for implements for counterfeiting, under the honest belief that circumstances called for it, it was held that this was evidence competent to go to the jury in mitigation of damages, when sued for committing the trespass. *Ib.*

d. In an action by the father of the girl, against the clerk for issuing a marriage license for the marriage of the daughter under eighteen, without the father's consent, evidence of the bad character of the husband is admissible to enhance damages. And evidence of the suitableness of the marriage is admissible in mitigation of damages. Larwell v. Kirby, 14 O. R. 1.

e. In an action against a sheriff for neglecting to sell property levied upon, and permitting it to become lost, the rule of damages is the value of the property. State v. Myers, 14 O. R. 438.

f. Where property is replevied from the custody of the sheriff, who holds it by virtue of a levy on execution, and a verdict is rendered in favor of the sheriff, the damages will be the full value of the property, unless that exceeds the amount due upon the execution, in which case the rule of damages will be the amount due on the execution. Jennings v. Johnson, 17 O. R. 154.

g. The rule of damages, in an action for a nuisance to a mill, is the actual damage sustained prior to the commencement of the suit. Prospective damages can not be recovered, nor damages to the permanent value of the mill-site, unless specially claimed in the declaration. Thayer v. Brooks, 17 O. R. 489.

h. L. covenanted with W. that he would not, for a specified time, be connected, either directly or indirectly, with the manufacture of stearine or star candles, in Hamilton county, Ohio, or at any other place in the United States, or give his assistance, or communicate his knowledge of the business to any person whatever, "under a forfeiture of four thousand dollars, as liquidated damages."

496 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 376, 379 TO 392.

*Held*, that the sum mentioned must be treated as liquidated damages, and not as a penalty. *Lange v. Werk*, 2 O. St. 519.

a. The measure of damages for the violation of an agreement to pay $1500 in wool at twenty cents per pound, is fifteen hundred dollars, and not the market value of the wool. *Trowbridge v. Holcomb*, 4 O. St. 38.

b. As a general rule, the opinion of a witness as to the amount of damages which the landholder will sustain by reason of the construction and use of a railroad, is not evidence. *Atlantic and Great Wes. R. R. Co. v. Campbell*, 4 O. St. 583.

c. When a contract is entered into for work at a stipulated price, if the right of election to pay for the same in money or specific articles at a certain rate or price, at the option of the debtor, exists, and the articles are not delivered, the plaintiff recovers the amount of the debt and interest; but if no such election is expressed or implied, the plaintiff is entitled to the market value of the articles, with interest. *Cleveland and Pittsburgh R. R. Co. v. Kelley*, 5 O. St. 180.

d. When, in an action for slander, the truth of the words spoken is pleaded in justification, in good faith, under an honest belief in their truth, and with reasonable grounds for such belief, the plaintiff is not, by reason of such plea, on the failure of proof to sustain it, entitled to exemplary damages, nor should it be regarded as an aggravation beyond the real injury sustained by the plaintiff. *Rayner v. Kinney*, 14 O. St. 283.

### SECTION 376.
### DOCKETING CAUSES.

e. *Cause stricken from docket can not be re-docketed.* There is no rule of law authorizing the issue of a rule against a party to show cause why a cause which had been stricken from the docket should not be re-docketed, or to re-docket the cause upon a return of such rule served. Wheat, C. J.: *Brown v. Ellington's adm'r*, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

### SECTIONS 379 to 392.
### WHEN CAUSE STANDS FOR TRIAL.

f. *Rendering judgment before day of trial erroneous.* The code of practice authorizes the court to render a judgment for so much of a demand sued for as is not controverted. But there is no provision of the code, or rule of practice, which authorizes the court, before an action is ready for final trial, to hear and try it, and render a judgment in favor of one of the parties (whose right to recover anything on the final hearing is not admitted), on the mere probability that he will be then entitled to the sum adjudged in his favor. *Mattingly v. Bosley*, 2 Met. 343.

g. *Same. Not a clerical error if objected to at time.* The court has no right to take a case up and hear it, except as provided for in the code, in opposition to the wishes of either party, until it stands regularly for hearing. And where the trial was premature and irregular, and objected to by the party at the time on that ground, and he then excepted to the action of the court, it was not necessary for him to make a subsequent motion to set the order of hearing and judgment aside; but he had a right to appeal therefrom directly to the court of appeals. *Ib.*

It details how trials can proceed when only some defendants have been served, the conditions for discontinuing actions against unserved defendants, and rules regarding default judgments when one defendant pleads payment. It also clarifies defenses that discharge an individual versus those that discharge the entire contract, and rules for when equitable actions are ready for trial, including provisions for change of venue.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 discusses rules of joinder in civil lawsuits. It clarifies that if a plaintiff sues some but not all jointly liable parties, and the non-joinder is not raised as a defense, the plaintiff can recover against those sued. It also states that if multiple defendants are sued on a joint contract but only a portion are liable, the plaintiff can recover against those who are actually liable. The text provides case examples to illustrate these principles, including a situation where a husband and wife were sued on a promissory note signed by the husband under their firm name, and the court allowed judgment against the husband alone.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil Code of Practice in 1867 and deals with procedural rules regarding the joinder of parties in civil lawsuits. It outlines how plaintiffs can proceed when not all jointly liable parties are included in the suit, and how judgments can be rendered against only those defendants found to be liable. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases discusses procedural rules for civil actions, specifically concerning joint liability in contract and tort cases. It outlines how a plaintiff can establish a cause of action against one or more defendants, even if not all defendants are found liable or if there's a misjoinder. It details scenarios where a plaintiff can recover against some defendants while others are acquitted or have the complaint dismissed as to them. The text references several case precedents to illustrate these points.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases provides notes and interpretations for Sections 400 and 402. Section 400 deals with the dismissal of civil suits, outlining conditions under which a court may dismiss a case, such as failure to obey orders or to make necessary parties to the suit. Section 402 addresses several judgments against joint defendants in civil actions, discussing the effects of releasing one joint trespasser, the possibility of separate judgments against summoned and unsummoned defendants in tort actions, and the implications of granting a new trial to one defendant while refusing it to others. It also covers scenarios involving joint obligors, including taking judgment against one and abating the case against another, and the erroneous nature of a default judgment against one joint obligor when a defense of payment by another obligor was raised. Finally, it distinguishes between defenses that discharge a person and those that discharge the obligation itself.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases. It details procedural rules for civil lawsuits, specifically concerning the dismissal of suits and the rendering of judgments against multiple defendants. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The rules appear to be general procedural guidelines applicable to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Civil Code of Practice provides notes and interpretations related to sections 404 and 405, which deal with the sale of incumbered property. It clarifies rules regarding sales subject to liens, how sales should be conducted, the conditions for sales (cash vs. credit), and grounds for setting aside sales. Notably, it specifies that sales of personal property or slaves made by order of court shall be on a credit of three months.

While the term 'slaves' explicitly refers to a group historically defined by race, the statute itself does not establish discriminatory practices based on race. Instead, it outlines procedural rules for sales, including a specific credit period for the sale of personal property or slaves. The mention of 'slaves' is a historical artifact of the time period and the legal context, rather than an active discriminatory provision within this specific procedural rule. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with an implicit racial indicator due to the mention of 'slaves' in a procedural context. 504 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 406.

judicial sales, ought to have a large influence in determining the exercise of the chancellor's discretion, in such cases. 2 B. Mon. 411; Todd v. Todd's adm'r., Ms. Opin., Winter Term, 1853. *Ib.*; Taylor v. Gilpin, 3 Met. 544.

a. *When sale should be set aside.* Land was sold at a commissioner's sale at a great sacrifice, and the directions of the judgment were not pursued by the commissioner in advertising the time and place of sale—*Held*, that the sale was properly disaffirmed by the chancellor. *Ib.*

b. *Purchaser merely an accepted bidder—his bid must be approved by court.* A purchaser of property under a decretal sale does not acquire any independent right by his purchase, until after the same has been approved by the chancellor. He is simply an accepted or preferred bidder; and whether his bid or proposal will be approved, depends upon the sound equitable discretion of the court having control of the cause. 3 Dana, 614; 2 B. Mon. 410; 5 *ib.* 494; Taylor v. Gilpin, 3 Met. 544.

c. *Inadequacy of price and irregularity in sale.* See the opinion for a case in which, on account of the inadequacy of price, and the peculiar circumstances attending the transaction, detailed in the opinion, a decretal sale was set aside upon a petition by the plaintiff filed before the sale was reported for confirmation by the commissioner. *Ib.*

d. *Sale made privately invalid.* Commissioner's sale made privately is invalid. The court has full power over commissioner's report until it is confirmed. Stites, J.: Dollins v. Leavell, Ms. Opin., June, 1856.

SECTION 406.

JUDGMENT IN REM AND PERSONAM.

e. *When plaintiff may have both.* A judgment enforcing a lien upon land, can be rendered *in personam* and *in rem* also. Stites, J.: Edwards v. Kendrick, Ms. Opin., December, 1856.

f. *Both must be sought in petition.* A judgment *in rem* can not be rendered unless the petition expressly prays such relief. Stites, J.: Landram v. Trimble, etc., Ms. opin., January, 1858.

g. *Plaintiff may proceed by execution or to enforce sale.* Under this section a judgment may be rendered *in rem* and *in personam*, and the plaintiff may collect his debt by execution, or by a sale of the lien property. Stites, J.: Ghiselin, etc. v. Sterritt, Ms. opin., October, 1856.

h. *May proceed to enforce both at same time.* Any rule of practice by which the assignee is prevented from proceeding at the same time to enforce his personal judgment and judgment *in rem*, should not be adopted. Chambers v. Keen, 1 Met. 289.

i. *Must proceed with diligence to secure claim upon assignor.* The 406th section of the Civil Code does not affect the rights and duties of the assignor and assignee; it only authorizes the personal judgment and judgment for foreclosure in the same action; it is still the duty of the assignee to cause his execution to issue on his personal judgment within a reasonable time; and a delay of nearly two months upon the part of the assignee to issue execution upon his personal judgment against the debtor, is not reasonable diligence, and he thereby forfeits his recourse upon the assignor. *Ib.*

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Kentucky Civil Code discusses the recording and amendment of judgments, and the protection of infants' rights in legal proceedings. It details requirements for judgments to be entered on the order book, signed by the judge, and the conditions under which records can be amended. It also outlines the court's duty to vacate unjust judgments against infants, even if procured by fraud, and that this can be done during their minority.

508 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 432 TO 437, 439.

of this section apply to all judgments, whether rendered before or after the code took effect. Stites, J.: Norris' adm'r v. Chafualt, Ms. Opin., January, 1857.

## SECTIONS 432 TO 437.

### DEATH OF PARTY AFTER JUDGMENT.

**a. Death of plaintiff suspends but does not abate judgment or execution.** The death of one or all the plaintiffs in a judgment or execution does not actually abate either the judgment or the execution, but merely suspends their operation until an administrator be appointed and the clerk rendered able to indorse his name upon the execution, by the filing of the proper affidavit. No proceeding in the nature of a *scire facias* is required, no summons issues against the defendant; nor is any order of the court necessary to enable the clerk to issue execution on the judgment. Morgan, etc. v. Winn's adm'r., 17 B. Mon. 244; and Venable v. Smith, 1 Duv. 195.

**b. One defendant dead—execution should issue against survivors.** Where judgment has been rendered against all the defendants, without noticing the death of one, it is proper for the court to order the execution to issue in the names of the survivors. Simpson, J.: Webb's adm'r v. Hill, Ms. Opin., January, 1856.

**c. Indorsement gives no validity till affidavit filed.** The indorsement upon an execution authorized by sec. 432, confers no right upon the administrator to enforce it, until the affidavit required by sec. 434 shall be filed with the clerk by one of the plaintiffs, and also the certificate of administration, etc., and a replevin bond taken under an execution without such affidavit and certificate, should be quashed upon motion. Stites, J.: Ardery's adm'r v. Tanner, Ms. Opin., December, 1857.

**d. Death of plaintiff does not abate judgment or execution.** The death of plaintiff abates neither the judgment nor execution, but suspends proceedings upon them until administration granted, and revivor had as authorized by secs. 432, 433, 434. 1 Duv. 195.

**e. Death of one of several defendants in a judgment does not prevent execution issuing against survivors.** Fleece v. Goodman, 1 Duv. 306.

**f. Where the record of a judgment has been destroyed, the court may upon motion after notice, supply the record upon proper evidence and award execution thereon against the survivors where part of the defendants are dead.** *Ib.*

## SECTION 439.

### DEFENDANTS CONSTRUCTIVELY SUMMONED.

**g. Unnecessary to prove execution of instrument purporting to be signed.** In an action against a non-resident defendant, constructively summoned, who does not appear, it is not incumbent on the plaintiff, under the requisition of sec. 439 of the civil code, to prove the execution of a promissory note which he holds upon such defendant, and which is the foundation of the action asserted in the petition. The note itself is *prima facie* evidence of indebtedness on the part of the defendant. Gill v. Johnson, 1 Met. 649.

**h. Same where instrument in hands of assignee.** In such case, although the plaintiff is not the payee of the note, but the holder of it by delivery without written transfer, the possession of it is *prima facie* evidence of ownership, and,

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Kentucky Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases details procedures related to the death of parties in civil judgments. It clarifies that the death of a plaintiff or defendant does not abate a judgment or execution but rather suspends or allows for continuation against survivors, outlining the necessary administrative steps and court orders. It also addresses the process of supplying destroyed judgment records and awarding execution against surviving defendants. A separate section (439) discusses constructive service on non-resident defendants and the evidentiary requirements for instruments like promissory notes in such cases.
classification.reasoning: The provided text from the 1867 Kentucky Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases deals exclusively with civil procedure, specifically the handling of judgments and executions when a party to the case dies, and the process for dealing with non-resident defendants. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of practice.

classification.title: Constructive Process - Attorney - Bond
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice (1867) outlines the requirements for rendering judgments against absent defendants in civil cases, particularly those involving attachments. It mandates the appointment of an attorney to defend for the absent defendant, the execution of a bond by the plaintiff to protect the absent defendant's interests, and specifies that failure to comply with these requirements can be grounds for reversal of the judgment. The section also clarifies that while failure to execute the bond might make the judgment erroneous, a sale made under such a judgment could still be valid. It also notes that an absent defendant appealing the case enters an appearance.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with civil procedure concerning absent defendants and the legal safeguards required before a judgment can be rendered against them. It focuses on procedural requirements like appointing an attorney and executing a bond to protect the absent party's rights. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of practice.

classification.title: DIVORCE - COST AND COUNSEL FEES
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 outlines provisions related to divorce, specifically addressing grounds for divorce, the sufficiency of evidence, and the awarding of alimony and costs. It details conditions under which a divorce may be granted, such as cruel and inhuman treatment or abandonment, and clarifies that a wife is not automatically entitled to alimony upon divorce, especially if her conduct contributed to the marital difficulties. The statute also mandates that husbands generally pay the costs of divorce and alimony suits, including the wife's attorney fees.

The category is 'marriage' as the statute directly pertains to divorce, which is a legal dissolution of marriage. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of practice.

classification.title: CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC.462. 513
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1867 discusses legal procedures related to divorce and alimony. It details the conditions under which a husband is liable for his wife's costs in divorce proceedings, the court's power to fix and enforce attorney's fees, and the restoration of property after a divorce. It clarifies that a wife failing to obtain a divorce is not automatically entitled to a judgment for her costs, and outlines how property disputes can be settled.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The statute deals with general civil legal matters concerning marital dissolution and financial settlements. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
514 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 465. 466.

a. *Stranger purchasing property pending suit for divorce.* Quere: Was sec. 462, *supra*, intended to apply to any case in which an issue as to the restoration of specific property is made by the pleadings in the divorce suit? It does not apply to a case in which a third person, claiming property of the wife, under a *pedente lite* purchase from the husband, causes himself to be made a party to the divorce suit in order to assert his claim. In such case, it is not necessary to postpone a trial of her suit for divorce until the issue between her and such claimant shall be ready for trial. *Ib.* 486.

b. *No attachment or injunction necessary.* As between the wife and husband, nor order of attachment or injunction is necessary, nor is it necessary that the property should be referred to in her petition for a divorce, to entitle her to an order for its restoration. *Ib.* 486.

c. *What sufficient notice of wife's right.* Actual notice to a purchaser from the husband, that the wife has sued for a divorce, and that she claims the property, brings the case within the statute which provides that sales and conveyances made by the husband to a purchaser with notice and with intent to defraud or hinder the wife, shall be void as to the wife. It does not mean notice of an order of attachment or injunction. *Ib.*

d. *Legislative divorce valid, and binds wife.* Where a legislative divorce was obtained in 1840–1, with the written assent of the wife, and she received a sum of money agreed upon at the time of separation, and acquiesced in the divorce for thirteen years, enjoying, in the mean time, the rights of a *feme sole*, a court of equity should not listen to her application to invalidate such divorce, and have dower. *Cabell v. Cabell*, 1 Met. 319.

## SECTIONS 465, 466.

### SETTLEMENT OF DECEDENT'S ESTATES.

e. *To what estates the statute and code applies.* The statute which regulates the administration of the estate of any person who was an inhabitant of any other state or country, as well as that which provides for the settlement and distribution of insolvent estates, by an action to be brought for that purpose by the administrator, contemplates the existence of personal estate, and its due and proper administration; and has no application to a case where the estate of the decedent does not pass into the hands of an administrator, and where no duties or liabilities can devolve upon him (Rev. Stat., p. 335; Civil Code, secs. 465, 466). *Thumb v. Gresham*, 2 Met. 306.

f. *Sec. 466 limited by sec. 539.* Sec. 466 of the civil code, which provides for the sale of slaves or real estate descended or devised to the heirs or devisees of a decedent, in an action for the settlement of his estate, where it appears that the personal estate is insufficient for the payment of debts, is limited, so far as it relates to the real estate of infant heirs and devisees, by sec. 539 of the code. *Gill v. Given*, 4 Met. 197.

g. *Sale of more of infant's estate than necessary, void.* So much of the real estate of an infant heir or devisee (under the sections, *supra*) as may be necessary to pay the debts of the ancestor or testator, may be sold in a proceeding to settle the decedent's estate; but if more than is necessary for that purpose be sold, except in the mode prescribed by the Revised Statutes, in chap. 86, the judgment ordering the sale, and the sale made thereunder, are void. *Ib.*

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 474 TO 478. 517

property found." In the meantime, the judgment is reversed by the court of appeals, and a different judgment rendered. P. sued out an attachment, alleging the return of "no property," upon the first judgment, and the fact that M. was still insolvent; *held*, not to authorize an attachment under sec. 474, Civil Code. Marshall, C. J.: *McGuire v. Parsons*, Ms. Opin., June, 1855.

a. *When plaintiff entitled to judgment against garnishee.* In a proceeding under sec. 474, it is necessary to state, or to charge, that a third person is indebted to the defendant in the execution, in order to take judgment by confession. It is not sufficient to state that plaintiff is *informed* that such is the case. Crenshaw, C. J.: *Lexington & Danville R. R. Co. v. Morrissey*, Ms. Opin., December, 1856.

b. *Lien created by attachment.* Upon the issual and levy of a general attachment obtained in an action by equitable proceedings by a judgment creditor against the debtor, upon a return of execution "no property found," a lien upon the property of the debtor levied on is created in behalf of the plaintiff to the extent of his debt, and he is entitled to its enforcement against all subsequent purchasers. *Lewis v. Quinker*, 2 Met. 284.

c. *No affidavit or bond required.* In such case no affidavit or bond by the plaintiff is required to be filed before the attachment issues. *Ib.*

d. *Judgment and return of no property basis of attachment.* The basis of the attachment in such case is the judgment and return of no property, which, when alleged, furnish *prima facie* evidence that the demand is still unpaid; but if the failure to allege in terms that the judgment is still unsatisfied be a material defect, it can be supplied by subsequent amendment to that effect, and will not then operate to vitiate the attachment or levy and lien. *Ib.*

e. *Levy of attachment—what sufficient.* Where the return of the officer shows that he levied the attachment upon a town lot of the defendant, and "posted a copy of the attachment on the premises, there being no tenant," the omission to state that the copy was left in a conspicuous place on the premises, is cured by the presumption that the officer did his duty, nothing appearing to the contrary. *Quere*: Does not the word "posted" supply the omission? *Ib.*

f. *What interest of defendant may be subjected.* Sec. 23 of chap. 80 of the Revised Statutes, rendering trust estates liable for the debts of beneficiaries, is but a re-enactment of the 13th section of the act of 1796 on the same subject (1 Digest of Stat. Law, 443). The adjudications of the court of appeals, construing the act of 1796, determine the purview of the section of the Revised Statutes, *supra*. Cases cited construing the act of 1796, 3 Bibb, 186; 3 J. J. Mar. 264; 8 B. Mon. 61; 12 *Ib.* 483; *Samuel v. Salter*, 3 Met. 260.

g. Sec. 474 of the Civil Code furnishes an effectual mode, upon the return of an execution *nulla bona*, of reaching the class of interests declared subject to the payment of debts by the section of the Revised Statutes, *supra*. *Ib.*

h. Property or funds can not be vested in trustees for the *use* of another, without subjecting it to the debts of the *cestui que trust*. *Ib.*

i. *Same.* An estate was devised to a trustee, with directions to use and control the same as he may think best, and out of the rents, hires, interest, proceeds, and profits, furnish the testator's son, from time to time, as he may need the same, such sums as may be sufficient for his reasonable and comfortable support during his life—the overplus of rents, hires, and profits to be divided

524 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 526.

but a proceeding to correct such settlement, should be decided upon exceptions taken and passed upon in the county court. Stites, J.: Pointer, etc. v. Curd, Ma. Opin., February, 1858.

a. *Undue influence by guardian over ward in settlement.* A receipt, even between parties standing in no relation of trust or confidence, may be explained, varied, or contradicted by parol evidence. Less effect will be given to a receipt executed by a ward to his guardian, and especially in a case where the minority of the ward has but recently ceased, and where, for that reason, the relation will be considered as still having an undue influence on the mind of the ward. Clay v. Clay, 3 Met. 548.

b. *Receipt in full by ward to guardian—not binding.* A receipt was executed to the guardian by his ward within less than a month after the latter had attained full age, upon a final settlement with the guardian, for a specific sum, in full of all claims, dues and demands against the guardian. The ward was the nephew of the guardian, and reposed the utmost confidence in his integrity and judgment. He had resided with his mother in a distant county, and had never heard of an allowance improperly made to the guardian in a settlement previously made with the county commissioners until after his final settlement with and receipt to his guardian—*Held*, that the receipt does not have the effect of a conclusive bar to a re-examination of the accounts of the guardian. *Ib.*

c. *Guardian using ward's funds liable for interest.* The equitable doctrine applicable to all trustees, and especially to guardians, is, that if they use the trust fund, or mingle it with their own moneys, they are liable for the fund and interest. *Ib.*

d. *Loaning ward's money without security.* A guardian whether appointed by will or under the statute, who loans the money of the ward without taking security therefor, will not be allowed to escape liability, by merely showing that the borrower was in the possession of a considerable estate, and was reputed to to be solvent, or even wealthy, at the time of the loan. The loan must be made on safe securities. *Ib.*

e. *Interest should be compounded every two years.* Upon settlement with a guardian, interest should be compounded every two years, up to the arrival of the ward at full age; and, as the relation of guardian and ward then ceases, current interest only should be allowed, upon the aggregate amount then due, until paid. *Ib.*

SECTION 526.

MANDAMUS.

f. *Not to be awarded against officer having a discretion.* The writ of mandamus should never be awarded to compel a judicial officer to perform, or abstain from performing, an act in regard to which he has a judicial discretion (Bacon's Abr. 436; 3 Black. Com. 110; 2 Strange, 881; 19 Johnson, 260; 5 Wendell, 122); and should generally be refused when the applicant has some other appropriate remedy. Goheen v. Myers, 18 B. Mon. 426.

g. *Same.* An appeal was taken from a justice's court to the quarterly court, upon a judgment for $8 65, and dismissed by the judge of the quarterly court for want of jurisdiction—*Held*, that the circuit court could not properly issue a mandamus to compel the quarterly court judge to reinstate the case on his

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 539. 527

person is constitutionally ineligible to the office of sheriff he will not be the lawful incumbent thereof, although he may be elected, obtain a certificate of his election from the examining board, take the oath of office, and execute the bond prescribed by law. Although his acts, as an officer *de facto*, are valid as to third persons, and can not be questioned collaterally, nevertheless they are invalid so far as he is himself concerned, and his mere color of title to the office will not avail him as a protection in actions against him for trespasses on person or property; but it may be proved that he was not a resident of the county at the time he was elected, and, therefore, ineligible.

a. *Certificate of examining board—effect.* The certificate which the examining board issues to a candidate that he is elected to the office of sheriff—although conclusive evidence that he was elected thereto, unless his election be contested before the proper board—is not even *prima facie* evidence that he was eligible to the office. *Ib.*

b. *County court can not inquire into eligibility of sheriff elect.* Although the county court is authorized by the statute in certain enumerated cases to declare the office of sheriff vacant, it has no power to inquire into the eligibility of a person claiming to be elected to the office, and applying to be qualified and execute bond, who presents a certificate of his election, or to refuse to permit him to qualify and execute bond according to law, upon the ground that he is ineligible to the office; and the fact that he has qualified and given an official bond in the county court as sheriff, can not be relied on to prove his eligibility to the office. *Ib.*

SECTION 539.

SALE OF INFANTS' ESTATES.

c. *Sales ordered before code took effect.* The provisions of the code regulating proceedings for the sale of the real property and slaves of infants, married women, etc., do not affect a sale, where the order directing it was made prior to the 1st of July, 1854. Stites, J.: *Fields v. Robinson*. Ms. Opin., October, 1855.

d. *Provisions do not apply to settlement of insolvent estates.* Revised Statutes, chap. 65, page 469, does not apply to commissioners of the circuit court, who sell land, slaves, etc., but applies only to cases involving the settlement of insolvent estates and accounts, and where the compensation is to be determined after the rendition of the service. Stites, J.: *Edwards v. Kendrick*, Ms. Opin., December, 1856.

e. *Guardian must answer—bond must be executed.* Where a suit is brought to sell the real estate of infants, it is necessary, in order that the sale may be valid as to them, that they should have a statutory guardian, and that he should appear and answer, and execute such a bond as the statute requires. It is not sufficient that the bond be executed after the sale takes place. *Wyatt, etc. v. Mansfield's heirs*, 18 B. Mon. 781.

f. *Commissioners must report.* The sale is also void if commissioners are not appointed to report, on oath, the net value of the infant's real and personal estate, and the annual profits thereof. Without such report, the court has no jurisdiction to decree the sale. *Ib.*

g. *Conditions precedent to sale.* The circuit court has no jurisdiction to order the sale of infants' real estate, until the guardian of each infant, whether plaintiff or defendant, executes the covenant to the infant required by the 2d section

classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases details procedural requirements for the sale of infants' real estate. It outlines conditions such as the execution of bonds by guardians, the sufficiency of reports from commissioners valuing the estate, and the necessity of demonstrating that the sale is in the best interest of the infants. It also addresses situations involving non-resident guardians and the sale of estates in remainder.

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases provides notes and interpretations related to Section 539, which deals with the sale of real estate belonging to infants. It outlines strict compliance requirements for statutes authorizing such sales, detailing what commissioners' reports must show (e.g., net value, annual profits), what the appointing order must require, the necessity of commissioners being sworn, and the requirement for the report to demonstrate that the infant's interest necessitates the sale. It also clarifies the relationship between Section 466 and Section 539 regarding the sale of real estate to pay ancestor's debts, distinguishing between cases where heirs are adults and infants.

580 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 543, 546.

thorizing the sales of the estates of infants, and in the decisions of this court on the statute, means voidable only. *Thornton v. McGarth*, 1 Duv. 349.

a. Where the record *fails to show that the commissioners were sworn*, to value the infants' estate, the court of appeals can not presume that they were not in fact sworn as the law requires. *Ib.*

b. The statement in the judgment that the guardian had given the requisite bond, authorizes the presumption that it was given before or simultaneously with the rendition of the judgment, although the date is of a subsequent day. *Ib.*

c. *The failure of infants to answer by guardian or otherwise*, is an error for which the judgment could be reversed, but did not render the judgment and sale in pursuance thereof void. *Ib.*

d. *The statutes of 1861-2 confirming defective sales* do not impair the obligation of contracts or divest vested rights, and are constitutional. *Ib.*

### SECTION 543.

### SALES OF CITY PROPERTY, ETC.

e. *When section 543 does not apply.* Where the proceedings for the sale of a house and lot in a town, belonging in part to infants, are framed and adopted with reference to the 86th chapter of the Revised Statutes, *supra*, their validity must be tested by the provisions of that chapter, and the provisions of the 543d section of the Civil Code do not apply. *Barber v. Hopewell*, 1 Met. 260.

f. *Irregularities under this section do not render sale void.* In a proceeding, under section 543 of the Civil Code, for the sale of a town lot held by tenants in common, some of whom are infants, the failure of the chancellor, before ordering the sale thereof, to observe the subsequent requisitions of that section, will not render the sale *void*, but only renders the judgment erroneous. *Todd v. Dowd's heirs*, 1 Met. 281.

g. *Purchaser can not resist payment on account of errors merely.* The purchaser of the property, at such sale, can not, after its confirmation by the chancellor, and after the expiration of the term at which such confirmation is made, resist the payment of the purchase money upon the ground of the irregularity of the proceedings under which the sale was had. *Aliter* where the sale was *void* (16 B. Mon. 289; 18 *Ib.* 387). *Ib.* 281.

h. *Nor can court revise its own judgment after sale made and confirmed.* Nor has the chancellor the power, in such case, to go behind the judgment confirming the sale for the purpose of revising the proceedings or of determining the validity of the sale; the error therein not coming within those enumerated in either of the subdivisions of section 579 of the Civil Code, for which causes alone such power is allowed (18 B. Mon. 97, 760. *Ib.* 281.

i. *Postponement of payment.* Nor will the fact that the chancellor, subsequent to the sale, ordered the postponement of the payment of the infants' shares of the purchase money until they became of age, etc., relieve the purchaser at the sale from the payment of the shares of the adult. *Ib.* 281.

### SECTION 546.

### DIVISION OF LAND.

j. *Administrator has no power to divide.* In view of the provisions of the Revised Statutes, art. 11, sec. 1, page 591, and section 546 of the Civil Code, it

entries.ocr_text:
532 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 575, 577, 578.

SECTION 575.

PROCEEDINGS TO REVERSE, VACATE AND MODIFY.

a. *Jurisdiction of the court of appeals.* The court of appeals have exclusive jurisdiction to reverse, vacate or modify a judgment or decree for "errors appearing in the record," although the proceedings in which the decree complained of was rendered were commenced before the code was adopted. Duvall, J.: *Morgan v. Simpson*, Ms. Opin., June, 1857.

b. *Errors must be prejudicial to the party complaining.* The court of appeals will not reverse a judgment on account of the error of the circuit court in refusing to strike out irrelevant or redundant matter in a pleading, unless it appears that the appellant was prejudiced thereby. *Buckles v. Lambert*, 4 Met. 330.

SECTIONS 577, 578.

CLERICAL MISPRISIONS.

c. *When court of appeals will reverse for.* The court of appeals can not reverse a cause for a clerical misprision until the circuit court refuse, on application, to correct it. An error of the clerk in misstating, in a judgment, the time when interest begins to run, is such a misprision. *Wilson, etc. v. Barnes*, 13 B. Mon. 332.

d. *Adjudging sale of non-resident's property before bond executed, error.* Making an order of sale of real property belonging to an absent defendant constructively summoned, without an affidavit of the plaintiff that the plaintiff has no personal property, or not enough to satisfy the demand known to him, is an error of the court, not a clerical error. *Payne v. Witherspoon*, 14 B. Mon. 271.

e. *Mistake in computing interest a misprision.* An error in the date from which interest is to be computed, in entering judgment upon a note or bill of exchange, or allowing costs of protest, will not be cause for reversal, unless the circuit court has refused to correct it on motion. *Clark, etc. v. Finnel, etc.*, 16 B. Mon. 334.

f. *Judgment when process served in wrong county, error, and not misprision.* A judgment rendered in a suit without process having been served in the county of defendant's residence, objected to by defendant, is not a clerical error, but an error of the court. *Raymon v. Reed*, 16 B. Mon. 350.

g. *Judgment against infants before defense made, error.* The rendition of a judgment before a defense by the guardian for the infant defendants was not a clerical misprision, or a mere hearing of the action before it stood for trial; but an error of the court, and such an error as is available for a reversal by the court of appeals. *Pond v. Doneghy, etc.*, 18 B. Mon. 561.

h. *Docketing motion in wrong name, a misprision.* The motion upon notice to the county attorney authorized by sec. 3, act 1851, page 362, should be docketed in the name of the commonwealth, but if docketed in the name of the county attorney, it is deemed a clerical misprision which will not operate to dismiss the appeal to this court in his name; the error should have been corrected by motion in the circuit court (See sec. 580). Simpson, J.: *Taylor's ex'or v. Barry, etc.*, Ms. Opin., September, 1855.

i. *Party resisting an order, it can not be treated as a misprision.* The failure of the appellant to treat the refusal of the court to continue a case as a clerical

classification.title: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases-1867
classification.summary: This section of the 1867 Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases outlines the jurisdiction of the court of appeals regarding the reversal, vacation, or modification of judgments and decrees. It details the types of errors that can be appealed, including those appearing in the record and clerical misprisions. It specifies that errors must be prejudicial to the complaining party and that clerical misprisions must first be brought to the attention of the circuit court for correction before they can be appealed. Examples of errors and misprisions are provided, such as incorrect interest calculations, improper sale of non-resident property, and judgments against infants before defense is made.

classification.title: SECTION 579. WHEN COURT MAY VACATE OR MODIFY ITS OWN JUDGMENT.
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice outlines the limited circumstances under which a court can vacate or modify its own judgments after the term in which they were rendered. It specifies that such actions are only permissible on grounds enumerated in Section 579, and not for general reasons like lack of proof or an unjust demand. The text also clarifies that a decree is only fraudulent if improper practice was used to obtain it, and a party who failed to make a defense will not be allowed to vacate a judgment on grounds of fraud after the term. The section further addresses judgments entered upon the mandate of an appellate court, stating that while the mandate is imperative, the resulting judgment can still be vacated or modified under the conditions specified in Sections 579 and 581.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a section from the Civil Code of Practice in Kentucky from 1867. It deals with the procedural rules for courts to vacate or modify their own judgments. The content focuses entirely on legal procedure and the limitations on judicial power after a term has ended. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of practice.

536 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 581, 588, 594.

instituted for such a purpose must be commenced within the time prescribed by the code. *Ib.*

a. *Same.* In *Rouse v. Williams*, *Ms. Opin.*, rendered at the present term, it was decided that a petition to vacate the judgment of the circuit court ought not regularly to be filed before the mandate of the court of appeals was entered up, yet, if it was so filed, and presented a sufficient cause for vacating or modifying the judgment of the circuit court, after the expiration of the term, it might be sustained. *Ib.* 775.

SECTION 581.

b. *Judgment can not be vacated on ex parte affidavit.* The *ex parte* affidavit of one party, to facts of which the other party is not presumed to have any knowledge, will not authorize a court to vacate or modify the judgment of a former term, no matter how good the grounds it contains may be. The appropriate and only mode is prescribed by sec. 581 of the Civil Code. Duvall, J.: *Lawson v. Estis*, *Ms. Opin.*, January, 1858.

SECTION 588.

PROOF OF EXHIBITS FILED WITH PLEADINGS.

c. *To what writings sec. 588 applies.* This section applies only to writings executed by one of the parties. Simpson, J.: *Breden v. Bettison*, *Ms. Opin.*, January, 1853.

d. *No proof necessary till genuineness denied.* No proof of the execution of a paper, which was apparently executed by the party against whom it is proposed to be read, is necessary, until its genuineness is denied by him, by his affidavit, before the trial is begun. Stites, J.: *Mayhugh v. McDowell*, *Ms. Opin.*, December, 1857.

e. *Same.* If a party relies upon a writing in his pleading and files it with the same, and it purports to be executed by the opposite party, it may be read as genuine against such party, unless he denies its genuineness by affidavit before the trial is begun. *Haney v. Tempest*, 3 Met. 96.

f. *Variance between petition and exhibit.* Suit upon a note, the petition alleging that it was payable to the plaintiff. The note filed with the petition was not payable to the plaintiff, but to another—*Held*, in the absence of an allegation of right in the plaintiff, by assignment or otherwise—that the petition is defective. *Ib.*

g. *The execution of deeds filed with the petition as the foundation of a proceeding in equity although not authenticated according to law, is admitted by failure of defendant to deny; and as to defendants constructively summoned who do not appear, proof of their execution is not necessary.* Barret v. Coburn, 3 Met. 510.

h. *A writing purporting to have been executed by the defendant, referred to in and filed with plaintiff's reply to his answer and cross-petition, may be read as genuine against him, unless he denies its genuineness by affidavit before the trial is begun.* Civil Code, sec. 588; *Wells v. Lewis*, 4 Met. 269.

SECTION 594.

i. *Traveling expenses to be tendered to witnesses.* The 594th and 614th secs. of the code do not divest witnesses residing out of the county in which the ac-

588 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 645.

that the witness was a practicing physician in order to entitle it to be read; proof of the fact upon its introduction is sufficient. Crenshaw, J.: McCoy v. Vangeison, Ms. Opin., December, 1855.

a. *Party may be examined without leave of court.* No order of court is necessary to take the deposition of a party to an action. General Laws, 1856, chap. 15, p. 3. Simpson, J.: Clemens v. Vail, Ms. Opin., December, 1856.

b. *Trial of issue out of equity.* Upon the trial of an issue out of equity oral testimony is always admissible, and depositions may be used unless the court otherwise direct, where oral testimony alone will be admissible. Savings Bank v. Benton, 2 Met. 240.

c. *In ordinary actions neither party can be required to give deposition.* None of the provisions of the Civil Code authorize the taking of the deposition of a party by his adversary in an action by ordinary proceedings. Musick v. Ray, 3 Met. 427.

d. *Party declining to read a deposition, adverse party may read it.* Where a party takes a deposition, or makes a cross-examination, and declines to read it upon the trial, it may be read by the other party. So, if a party have a witness summoned and sworn upon the trial, but declines to examine him, the other party may examine him. In such case, the deposition, cross-examination or testimony of the witnesses is the evidence of the party who reads the deposition or cross-examination, or examines the witness, and he will not be permitted to impeach him. *Ib.*

e. *Party taking his adversary's deposition, thereby acquires no right to testify himself.* A party may take the deposition of his adversary if he choose to give it, and it will then be treated as any other deposition, but will confer no right on the party taking it to testify for himself in relation to any new matter stated in such deposition. The privilege of testifying in his own behalf is conferred by the Civil Code only when new matter is introduced, not responsive to his inquiries, in answer to his interrogatories annexed to his pleadings. *Ib.* 427.

f. *Adjournment to another place.* Where it was shown by the affidavit of the party that the witness was physically unable to attend at the place fixed by the notice to take his deposition, and the examiner adjourned the taking to a more convenient place and to another day, and forthwith posted notice thereof upon the front door of his office, and the deposition was taken in pursuance of the adjournment, and the facts, together with the affidavit upon which the adjournment was made, were certified by the examiner—*Held*, that the deposition could be read. Price v. Caperton, 1 Duv. 207.

SECTION 645.

CERTIFICATE.

g. *Certificate must show who wrote the deposition.* Exceptions to a deposition will be sustained, if the examiner's certificate fails to show by whom the deposition was reduced to writing. It can appear in no other way. Stites, J.: Ware v. Berry, Ms. Opin., October, 1857.

h. *And that deposition was read to, and subscribed in examiner's presence.* Where an officer to his certificate of a deposition taken before him, fails to state by whom the deponent's statements were reduced to writing, and that the same were read to, and subscribed by, the deponent in his presence, the deposition

It covers when a party may be examined without court leave, the trial of issues out of equity, the inability of parties to be compelled to give depositions in ordinary actions, the right of an adverse party to read a deposition declined by the other party, the implications of taking an adversary's deposition, and rules for adjourning deposition proceedings. It also details requirements for the examiner's certificate, including who wrote the deposition and that it was read to and subscribed by the deponent in the examiner's presence.

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SECS. 646, 649, 650, 651. 589

should be excluded. Simpson, J.: Collins v. McCallister, Ms. Opin., January, 1856.

a. *Certificate can not be amended except by leave of court.* No alteration or amendment in the certificate of the officer taking a deposition is allowable after it has been filed, without the previous authority of the court entered of record. Hall v. Renfro, 3 Met. 51.

b. *Same.* Where exceptions to a deposition had been sustained two years before the trial, the deposition withdrawn, and certificate amended without leave of the court, or knowledge of the opposite party, and then placed in the papers of the cause after the parties had announced themselves ready for trial, exceptions to the deposition, taken after the jury were sworn, were properly sustained. *Ib.*

SECTION 646.

c. *Examiner must deliver deposition to clerk.* A deposition was excepted to in the circuit court, on the ground that it was filed in the clerk's office by the party on whose side it had been taken. It was sealed when delivered to the clerk, but not having been delivered to him by the officer who took it, according to the directions of the code, it was rejected by the court—*Held*, that the deposition was properly rejected. Breeding, etc. v. Stamper, 18 B. Mon. 177.

SECTION 649.
[See note to sec. 645.]

SECTION 650.

d. *Record must show that exceptions were filed.* The indorsement of grounds of exceptions to depositions on the back of the same, unaccompanied with some entry of record showing that it was filed as part of the exceptions, is not such an entry of record as is required by the code. Stites, J.: Kilgore v. Davis, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

SECTION 651.
EXCEPTIONS.

e. *Must be filed, etc., before commencement of trial.* Exceptions to depositions in suits at law, for any other cause than the incompetency of the witness, or the irrelevancy or incompetency of the testimony, must be filed and noted of record before the commencement of the trial. Estham v. Curd, 15 B. Mon. 105.

f. *Evidence heard upon trial of exceptions must be shown by bill of exceptions.* Although depositions may have been excepted to, and a judgment overruling those exceptions also excepted to, yet, if no bill of exceptions discloses the testimony heard upon the exceptions, this court can not disturb the opinion of the lower court. Crenshaw, J.: Howerton v. Hamilton, Ms. Opin., January, 1856.

g. *Otherwise where error appears on face of record.* This court will correct for errors excepted to appearing on the face of the depositions. *Ib.*

h. *Exceptions too late after cause submitted.* Exceptions to depositions come too late, after an equity cause has been submitted and an interlocutory decree rendered. Such practice is inconvenient and ought not to be tolerated. Simpson, J.: Buren's ex'ors v. Wickliffe, Ms. Opin., December, 1856.

542 CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 670.

without leave of the court. Stites, J.: Kilgore v. Davis, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

a. *Court may permit witness to be recalled.* The judge may, or not, in his discretion, allow a witness to be recalled after argument commences. This discretion ought, however, to be judiciously exercised, and will not be interfered with, unless abused to the prejudice of the party complaining. Stites, J.: Martin v. Maury, Ms. Opin., January, 1858.

SECTION 670.

COMPETENCY OF WITNESSES—PARTIES TO THE RECORD AND TO THE ISSUE.

b. *Defendant may examine plaintiff, or his attorney, on motion to quash attachment.* Where there is an attachment upon a proceeding by ordinary petition, the defendant may examine the plaintiff on a motion to quash the attachment, and so may plaintiff's attorney. And where it has been conducted by both parties, no objection for want of notice is available. Talbot v. Pierce, 14 B. Mon. 197.

c. *Parties to record competent unless interested.* Before the adoption of the code, parties to the record in suits, were not, in general, competent witnesses for each other; by the code they are made competent, unless interested in behalf of themselves or others united with them in the issue. Allen's executor v. Shelby, 14 B. Mon. 321.

d. *Two sued—judgment against one—he is competent for the other.* Under the code, where two are sued and judgment is rendered against one, he is then a competent witness for the other defendant. *Ib.* 321.

e. *Party to the record competent unless interested.* The fact that the witness was a party to the record, did not render him incompetent. The law upon this subject has been changed by the code, and those only who are interested in the issue, on behalf of themselves, are excluded as witnesses. Unless, therefore, the witness was interested in the trial of the issue, he was not incompetent. Todd v. Luckett, 18 B. Mon. 128.

f. *Party examined, by his adversary, not required to criminate himself.* A party by being made a witness of his adversary, is not deprived of any privilege belonging to other witnesses. He will not be compelled to answer a question, when the answer might criminate himself, or have a tendency to expose him to a penal liability. This rule applies even in a suit in equity (Story's Equity Pleading, secs. 524, 576, 592; Mitford's Equity Plead. 157, 163); and under the provisions of the Code of Practice (sec. 143), a defendant is not required to verify his answer by affidavit, where the admission of the truth of the allegations of the petition, might subject him to a criminal or penal prosecution. Cole v. Wilson, 18 B. Mon. 216.

g. *Party may be examined without leave of court.* The deposition of a party to a suit may be read therein, without an order of court to take it, whenever, for want of interest, he would be a competent witness under the 669th and 670th sections. Simpson J.: Hardin v. Stone, Ms. Opin., December, 1853.

h. *Party to the issue incompetent whether interested or not.* That a person is a party to the issue, of itself renders him incompetent to testify either for himself or in behalf of those who are united with him in the issue, and his competency

CIVIL CODE—NOTES TO SEC. 670. 548

does not depend upon the question of interest. Chenoweth v. Fielding, 2 Met. 517.

a. *Persons interested incompetent whether party or not.* Persons are not incompetent as witnesses merely because they are parties to the action; but if they are parties to the issue, then they are incompetent to testify either in their own favor or in favor of those united with them in the issue; or, if they are not parties to the issue, yet if they are interested in it, whether they are parties to the action or not, they are also incompetent to testify in there own favor. *Ib.*

b. *Same defense though separate answers.* Defendants who rely upon the same matters of defense, although by separate answers, are parties to the issue, and one can not testify in behalf of the other, even where not incompetent on the score of interest. *Ib.*

c. *Trustee to establish instrument creating trust.* One who is constituted by a will a mere trustee for one of the devisees is a competent witness to prove facts necessary to establish the instrument. Montgomery v. Perkins, 2 Met. 448.

d. *Action for lost baggage—plaintiff not competent.* In an action to recover for the loss of a valise and its contents which had been placed in the custody of the defendant at his tavern by the plaintiff as a guest, the latter is not a competent witness to prove the contents of the valise or their value. Civil Code, sec. 670; Packard v. Northcraft, 2 Met. 439.

[Otherwise now; see amendment to sec. 70.]

e. *Drawer and indorser in favor of each other.* The drawer and the two indorsers of a bill of exchange, sued jointly by the holder, answered severally, and each denied the execution of the bill. Upon the trial each indorser was offered by his co-defendants as a witness to prove facts conducing to sustain their defense—*Held*, that the indorsers were competent witnesses. Smith v. Northern Bank, 1 Met. 575.

COMPETENCY OF WITNESS—INTEREST.

f. *Declarations made by an assignor*, of his property, for the payment of his debts, are not competent testimony against the beneficiaries. Simpson, J.: Newland v. Durkelsfield; Crabb v. Same, Ms. Opin., September, 1855.

g. *Grantor against grantee and in favor of grantor's creditor.* N. made a mortgage to L. & Co. A suit is instituted to subject the estate of L., who is one of the firm of L. & Co., to the payment of the debt of N. to L. & Co., upon the promise of L. to pay the said debt out of the mortgaged property, which he, L., had appropriated to his own use—*Held*, that N. has a direct interest, and is incompetent to testify. Stites, J.: Lee's ex'or v. Reese & Dobyns, Ms. Opin., September, 1855.

h. *Surety in favor of principal.* A surety in any bond executed to secure the payment of any judgment, the one might obtain against another, is interested, and incompetent. Simpson, J.: Borders v. Ray, Ms. Opin., September, 1857.

i. *Agent in favor of principal.* The master of a steamboat, who has no interest in the boat, is a competent witness where the owners are sued for damages for the death of a negro, caused by their failure to provide such necessary safeguards, for the preservation of human life, as are required by law.
