# APPEALS. 737

that John Smith has this day taken an appeal in the manner and form prescribed by law, from a judgment of the Franklin circuit court, rendered at its October term, 1854, in the case of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, plaintiff, against John Smith, defendant, for the confinement of the defendant in the state penitentiary at hard labor for the period of four years; it is therefore ordered that the execution of said judgment be suspended.

Witness my hand, this 10th day of October, 1854. J. SWIGERT, Clerk.

## Order Granting an Appeal in Cases of Misdemeanor.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,
*against*
John Smith, Defendant.

The defendant prayed an appeal from the judgment herein to the court of appeals, which is granted, upon condition that he lodge in the clerk's office of said court a transcript of the record in sixty days from the date of the judgment.

[The same form may be used where the appeal is prayed by the plaintiff.]

## Supersedeas Bond in Cases of Misdemeanor.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,
*against*
John Smith, Defendant.
Franklin Circuit Court.

The defendant, John Smith, having prayed an appeal from the judgment of the Franklin circuit court, rendered herein against him at its October term, 1854, for a fine of one thousand dollars and costs, and imprisonment in the jail of Franklin county for six months, now we covenant to and with the plaintiff that in case said judgment is affirmed, we will pay said fine and costs, and all damages thereon, and the costs of the appeal, and that the defendant will surrender himself in execution of the judgment of imprisonment; or, if he fails to do so, that we will pay to the plaintiff two dollars for each day of the imprisonment adjudged.

Witness our hands, this 20th day of October, 1854. JOHN SMITH,
THOMAS JONES,
GEORGE SMITH.

[The defendant need not sign the bond. It is sufficient if signed by the sureties.]

## Supersedeas in Cases of Misdemeanor.

John Smith, Appellant,
*against*
The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Appellee.
Court of Appeals of Kentucky.

I, Jacob Swigert, clerk of the court of appeals of Kentucky, do certify that the appellant, John Smith, has taken an appeal from a judgment of the Franklin circuit court, rendered at its October term, in favor of the Commonwealth of Kentucky against him for a fine of one thousand dollars and costs, and for his imprisonment in the Franklin county jail for six months, and has executed a supersedeas bond agreeably to law, a transcript of which, and of the record, has been filed in my office. Therefore, the execution of judgment is suspended.

Given under my hand this 1st day of November, 1854. J. SWIGERT, Clerk.

47

# CLAIMS ON THE TREASURY.

[The following forms and instructions were prepared by THOS. S. PAGE, Esq., Auditor of State, for the benefit of clerks and others having claims against the treasury, and for convenience are here inserted. To enable the Auditor to make up his files without confusion or difficulty, each return, order, or claim, should be upon a separate paper.]

## ATTORNEYS.

**SECTION 3.** In the absence of the commonwealth's attorney, at any term, or part of a term of a circuit court in his judicial district, the judge of such court shall appoint some suitable attorney to act in his place during his absence. He shall enter up an order making a reasonable allowance, to be paid out of the public treasury, to the person so appointed, and the same shall be deducted from the annual salary of the commonwealth's attorney; a copy of which order shall be forwarded by the clerk of the court to the auditor of public accounts.

## CLERKS.

Clerks are allowed for *all necessary well bound books, cases, and necessary presses* furnished for the use of their offices—the account to be made out and allowed by court, and certified to the auditor for payment, as follows, viz:

State of Kentucky, Hardin Circuit, Sct.:
May Term, 1842.

The following account for necessary well-bound books, was produced to court by Samuel Haycraft, clerk of the Hardin circuit court, viz:

*The Commonwealth of Kentucky,*
To S. Haycraft, Clerk of Hardin circuit court, Dr.
January 6, 1842. To one six-quire medium record book, *well bound*, - $14 00
May 6, 1842. To one six-quire execution book, *well bound*, - - 16 00
[And so on, for any thing else furnished.]
$30 00

Amounting to thirty dollars, which was sworn to by said clerk, allowed, and ordered to be certified to the auditor for payment.

Attest: SAMUEL HAYCRAFT, C. H. C. C.

[Accounts of a similar nature for other courts, to be made out and certified in the same manner.]

The allowance to county court clerks for commissioners' books, is one cent for each line to be calculated and allowed by the auditor; hence, no order of court is necessary.

Circuit and county courts are allowed a sum not exceeding ten dollars, for a seal of office, to be paid by the clerk, and allowed a credit therefor in settlement of tax account. An order showing the fact is sufficient.

(743)

It details the presentation, swearing, allowance, copying, and certification of accounts to the county court before payment. It also specifies allowances for recording births, deaths, and marriages. Additionally, it describes the procedure for constables to make out accounts for services rendered, which must also be allowed, copied, and certified by the clerk for payment. Examples are provided for both commissioners of tax and constables, including specific fees for tasks like taking lists of taxable property and apprehending individuals charged with felonies.

classification.title: IDIOTS
classification.summary: This statute outlines the legal process for declaring an individual an 'idiot' (a term used then for someone with severe intellectual disability) and appointing a committee to manage their care and finances. It details the court proceedings, jury's role in determining the individual's mental state, age, estate, and residency, and the appointment and responsibilities of a committee to provide for their support. It also specifies the financial arrangements for the individual's care, to be paid from the treasury and certified to the auditor.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal and financial management of individuals deemed 'idiots' in 1867 Kentucky. The language used ('idiot', 'unsound mind', 'destitute of mind from infancy') reflects the terminology of the time for severe intellectual disabilities. There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are applied generally to individuals meeting the criteria for 'idiocy', regardless of their race. Therefore, it does not appear to be a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled 'Code of Practice'.

still alive in the care of her committee, and has not sufficient estate for her support, and that her parents are still unable to support her—it is ordered that the allowance of fifty dollars per annum be continued from the last payment, and that the same be certified to the auditor.

A copy—Attest: MARSHALL KEY, C. M. C. C.

The court may order a new inquisition, or direct the idiot to be brought into court at any time when application is to be made for an allowance. When an idiot removes from one county to another, it will be necessary that the order of court, finding of the jury, etc., should be copied and forwarded to the place of removal, all of which must be copied and forwarded to the auditor, with the allowance afterward ordered, before any payment can be made.

A new inquest is required in 1855, and every five years thereafter. See *Revised Statutes*.

When an idiot dies, and the parents are unable to pay funeral expenses, the court can make an allowance not exceeding ten dollars for that purpose, which must be copied and certified to the auditor for payment.

See *Revised Statutes*, page 399, section 12.

The preceding forms are to be attested by the clerk of the circuit court, and certified as correct.
To enable the auditor to make separate files of the foregoing reports, orders, etc., the clerks will so make them out that they may be severed at pleasure.
License tax received by sheriffs to be reported. *See Revised Statutes*.
Additional revenue to be reported. *See Revised Statutes*.

MILITARY CLAIMS PAID OUT OF THE TREASURY.
Brigade inspectors are allowed $1 50 per day for attending regimental and battalion musters; account for services rendered, together with the brigadier's certificate for a faithful discharge of the duties assigned them, to be approved by the governor, and paid by the treasurer upon warrant from the auditor.
Judge advocates allowed $2 per day.
Provost marshals allowed $1 per day.
Witnesses allowed fifty cents per day, mileage, etc., as other witnesses.
These claims must be certified by the president of the court martial, giving the style of the court (whether general, division or brigade court martial, or court of inquiry).

SERGEANT AND TIPSTAFF OF THE COURT OF APPEALS.
The allowance to the sergeant of the court of appeals must be presented to court, allowed, copied, and certified before payment, viz:

The accounts for tipstaff of the court of appeals, to be made out in the same form.

SHERIFFS.

The allowances to sheriffs to be made out, presented to court, sworn to, allowed, copied, and certified in the same forms as before recited. See *Jailors, Constables, etc.*

Sheriffs to return delinquent lists on or before the third day of the winter term of the Franklin circuit court—form the same as the form of the commissioner's books—blanks can always be found with the clerks of the county courts; the delinquent lists to be presented to the county court, sworn to and allowed, and ordered to be certified to the auditor.

*Exonerations.*—Claims coming under this head arise from errors made by the commissioners of the revenue, and it is only necessary for the sheriff to have the facts stated by the clerk of the county court, under the directions of the court, and a credit will be entered.

SLAVES EXECUTED.

Before any thing can be drawn from the treasury for a slave that may be hung, it shall be the duty of the circuit court, "when they determine that any slave shall suffer death agreeably to law, to value such slave; and the auditor is authorized and required to issue his warrant on the treasury for the amount, in favor of the owner, on his producing to said auditor the certificate of the clerk of said court, that said slave was condemned, and the value stated, and the sheriff's certificate that said slave was executed, or perished before execution, and after conviction, and the treasurer is required to pay the same. Any slaves brought to this State for merchandise, or shall be passing through this State, by land or water, to any other State or territory, who may commit felony, for which they are tried and executed by the laws of this commonwealth, shall not be paid for out of the public treasury."

SUITS, LIST OF.

See *Revised Statutes*, page 180, section 5.
entries.citation: The Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases for the State of Kentucky, 1867, p. 777

classification.title: CLAIMS ON THE TREASURY.
classification.summary: This section outlines the procedures for claims against the Kentucky treasury, including accounts for court officials, sheriffs, and compensation for the execution of slaves. It details the required documentation, oaths, and certifications for payment. Specifically, it states that compensation for a slave executed will be paid to the owner upon proof of conviction and execution, but not if the slave was brought into the state for merchandise or passing through and committed a felony.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'slaves' in the context of compensation for execution. While this is a direct reference to enslaved people, and thus implicitly to Black individuals in the historical context of the United States, the statute itself does not establish discriminatory practices or separate facilities characteristic of Jim Crow laws. It is a procedural statute regarding compensation for property (in this case, a slave) that was legally executed. The mention of slaves is a remnant of the institution of slavery, which was being dismantled around the time of this code, rather than a proactive measure to enforce racial segregation or discrimination in the manner of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law, but the racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the direct mention of 'slaves'.

NATURALIZATION FORMS. 755

**WITNESSES.**

Witnesser for the commonwealth in criminal prosecutions will be allowed and paid when the claims are presented in the following form, viz:

*State of Kentucky, Madison Circuit Court, Sct.)*
June Term, 1842.

Ordered, that John Williams be allowed four dollars and fifty cents for nine days' attendance, as a witness, in behalf of the Commonwealth *vs.* Sam Jones, charged with murder: which is sworn to, and ordered to be copied and certified to the auditor for payment.

Attest: DAVID IRVINE, C. M. C. C.

When mileage, ferriages, and tollage are allowed, it will be necessary that the order shall show the number of miles traveled to and fro, and also the amount of ferriages and tolls, all of which must be sworn to.

**WOLVES, WILD CATS, AND RED FOXES KILLED.**

Any person claiming the benefit of the act for killing wolves, etc., shall produce before some justice of the peace for the county where such wolf, etc., was killed, the head thereof, and the justice shall administer to such person the following oath, to-wit: "You do solemnly swear [or affirm, as the case may be], that the skin [or scalp] and head now produced by you, is the skin [or scalp] and head of a wolf [cat, or fox], which you have killed in this State, and that you did not take this wolf [cat, or fox] in any other State or territory, and bring the same into this State, either directly or indirectly;" and said justice shall require, upon oath, *the time, as near as may be*, when said wolf was killed, and in what county; and such justice shall grant to the killer, a certificate stating his name, that he has killed a wolf, etc., and when killed, and that the killer has taken the oath required by the acts of assembly. The oath or certificate to be produced to the circuit court, who will allow the same, and direct it to be copied and certified to the auditor for payment.

**NATURALIZATION FORMS.**

**FORM OF DECLARATION OF INTENTION TO BECOME A CITIZEN.**

*State of Kentucky, Sct.*

At a court held for the Mason county court, at the court-house in the city of Maysville, on the 13th day of December, 1858.

This day, Patrick O'Flanagan, an alien, personally appeared in court, and upon his solemn oath, made the following declaration of his intention to become a citizen of the United States; that he came to the United States on the 20th day of January, 1856, from the county of Donnegal, in Ireland; that it is his *bona fide* intention to become a citizen of the United States, and to renounce and abjure all allegiance and fidelity to any foreign prince, potentate, State, or
